1:
On Luke 24:1 — “upon the first day of the week, very early in the morning, they came unto the sepulchre, bringing the spices which they had prepared” — the witnesses open the account of the resurrection morning.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the devotion and the design. Bede: the women, “as soon as the liberty of working returned,” bought spices to anoint the body, yet “as long as night-time restrained them, they came not.” Athanasius marks why the Lord rose on the third day and not at once: “in order… to show His body to be dead” — lest any say “He was never dead.” Cyril notes that “woman, who was once the minister of death, is now the first to… tell the awful mystery of” the resurrection. The grammatical-historical Robertson glosses “at early dawn” (orthrou batheos, “deep dawn”), with John 20:1 adding “while it was yet dark.”

The Puritan Henry distinguishes mystery from fact: the manner of the soul’s re-uniting with the body is hidden, but “the infallible proofs… that he did indeed rise… are things revealed.”

So the witnesses open the gospel’s climax in the quiet faithfulness of the women. They come at first light, the Sabbath past, bearing spices for a burial — expecting a body to anoint, not a Lord to meet. The witnesses note the fathers’ threads: that the third-day rising proves the death was real, sealing both the truth of his dying and the wonder of his rising; and that, fittingly, the women who loved him are the first witnesses, the sex once first in transgression now first in the news of life. They mark the devotion that brought them, in the dark, to a tomb they thought sealed; and the great surprise that awaits them. The verse thus sets the stage for the empty tomb and the angels’ word, and for the slow, joyful dawning of faith in a resurrection none of them had expected, though the Lord had plainly foretold it.

2:
On Luke 24:2 — “And they found the stone rolled away from the sepulchre” — the witnesses mark the removed stone.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the fact and its source. Robertson: “rolled away” (apokekylismenon, perfect passive), a rare verb. Ellicott suggests Luke’s narrative is “less vivid… than St. Mark’s” because his report may have come not from one of the Marys but from Joanna or Susanna. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: an angel had done this before they reached the tomb (Matthew 28:2); “when persons have strong confidence in God, obstacles do not hinder them… the removal of them they leave to him.” Gill recalls their concern on the way, “who should roll away the stone for them.”

The verse records the first sign that meets the women. The great stone Joseph had set to secure the tomb — the very obstacle they had worried over as they came — is found already rolled away. The barrier they could not move has been removed for them.

So the witnesses see in the rolled-away stone both a fact and a parable. The women came anxious how they should move so great a stone; God had moved it before they arrived. The witnesses note, with Clarke, the lesson for faith: those who set out in obedience to do what God requires may leave the obstacles to him, who removes them in his own way. The stone was not rolled back to let the risen Lord out — he needed no opening — but to let the witnesses in, that they might see the empty grave and believe. The verse thus marks the threshold of the great discovery: the seal of death broken, the way to the tomb opened, and the women drawn from their fear over the stone to the far greater wonder of the body gone. What they dreaded would block them becomes the door to the gospel of the resurrection.

3:
On Luke 24:3 — “And they entered in, and found not the body of the Lord Jesus” — the witnesses mark the empty grave.

The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that the words “of the Lord Jesus” are bracketed by some editors as a “Western non-interpolation,” one of several in this chapter. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke observes the precision: the evangelist “mentions the body particularly, to show that this only was subject to death,” the soul having been in Paradise (Luke 23:43). Gill notes the women entered, the Jewish tombs being “large enough for persons to go into.”

The verse gives the heart of the discovery. The women enter the tomb and find no body. The place where the Lord had been laid is empty; the grave that held him holds him no longer.

So the witnesses dwell on the bare and astonishing fact: the body of the Lord Jesus is gone. The witnesses note Clarke’s careful point that only the body had died — the soul lived, in Paradise, from the moment of his death — and now even the body is no longer in the place of death. The empty tomb is the first evidence of the resurrection, though the women do not yet understand it; they find absence where they expected to find a corpse to anoint. The witnesses observe that this emptiness is not loss but triumph: the grave could not keep him. Yet at this moment it brings only perplexity, for the women have no thought of a rising. The verse thus sets the empty tomb before us as the silent witness to the resurrection — a fact that will be interpreted, in the next verses, by the angels who declare that the One they seek among the dead is alive.

The witnesses note Clarke’s point that only the body had died — the soul lived, in Paradise — and now even the body is no longer in the place of death. The empty tomb is the first evidence of the resurrection, though the women do not yet understand it; they find absence where they expected a corpse. 4:
On Luke 24:4 — “And it came to pass, as they were much perplexed thereabout, behold, two men stood by them in shining garments” — the witnesses mark the angelic appearance.

The grammatical-historical commentators address the harmony. Ellicott notes that Mark and Matthew “mention one only,” and that “each set of informants” recorded what they saw. Robertson observes Luke has “men, not women” (andres duo), where Mark has “a young man” and Matthew “an angel.” Wesley reconciles: “two angels in the form of men… St. Matthew and Mark mention only one of them.” Gill notes the women’s perplexity over the missing body, “for they had no thought… of a resurrection.”

The verse brings the heavenly messengers into the women’s bewilderment. As they stand perplexed at the empty tomb, two men in dazzling garments suddenly appear beside them — angels, come to interpret the emptiness they cannot understand.

So the witnesses see the angels meet the women’s confusion with revelation. The empty tomb alone left them perplexed; the angels come to declare its meaning. The witnesses address the apparent discrepancy among the Gospels — one angel or two — explaining it as the natural variation of different witnesses recording what each beheld, with Luke and John noting the two and Mark and Matthew the one who spoke. They mark the shining garments as the token of heavenly glory, fitting messengers of the resurrection. The verse thus turns the scene from bewilderment toward understanding: into the women’s honest perplexity, where they grope for an explanation of the missing body, heaven sends its own interpreters. The angels’ word, in the verses to follow, will lift the women from confusion to the first proclamation of the risen Christ — that the One they seek is not here, but risen.

5:
On Luke 24:5 — “and as they were afraid, and bowed down their faces to the earth, they said unto them, Why seek ye the living among the dead?” — the witnesses mark the angels’ rebuking question.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the question. Ellicott: better “Him that liveth” — “the Lord whom they came to honour as dead was in very deed living… alive for evermore” (Revelation 1:18); the question “was enough to change the whole current of their thoughts.” Clarke notes it was “a common form of speech” for those “absurdly employed.” Robertson: “they had utterly forgotten the prediction of Jesus that he would rise.”

The verse gives the angels’ gentle reproof. Why look for the living among the dead? The women seek a corpse in a tomb; but the One they seek is alive. The question itself contains the gospel — he is not dead but living.

So the witnesses see the angels turn the women’s grief by a single searching question. They came seeking the dead, with spices for a body; the angels tell them they seek in the wrong place, for the Lord is not among the dead but alive. The witnesses note Ellicott’s point that the question was “enough to change the whole current of their thoughts” — lifting them at once from the search for a corpse to the wonder of a living Lord. They mark the women’s fear and reverence, the bowed faces, before the heavenly visitants. The verse thus delivers the first intimation of the resurrection in the form of a question that rebukes their forgetfulness and redirects their hope. Christ is “He that liveth,” alive for evermore; and the women, who came to mourn the dead, are summoned to seek the living — the whole posture of their hearts overturned by the angels’ word.

So the witnesses see the angels turn the women’s grief by a single searching question. They came seeking the dead, with spices for a body; the angels tell them they seek in the wrong place, for the Lord is alive. The witnesses note Ellicott’s point that the question was “enough to change the whole current of their thoughts” — lifting them at once from the search for a corpse to the wonder of a living Lord. They mark the women’s fear and bowed faces before the heavenly visitants. Christ is “He that liveth,” alive for evermore.
6:
On Luke 24:6 — “He is not here, but is risen: remember how he spake unto you when he was yet in Galilee” — the witnesses mark the proclamation and the appeal to memory.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both halves. Robertson: “He is not here, but is risen” — another Western non-interpolation, though genuine in Mark 16:6 and Matthew 28:6; the third-day rising Jesus “plainly foretold” (Luke 9:22; Luke 18:33), “and yet they had forgotten it.” Ellicott marks that the “direct appeal to the memory of the women is peculiar to St. Luke,” showing they too had heard the prophecies of the Passion (Luke 9:43).

The verse gives the angels’ great announcement and its ground. “He is not here, but is risen” — and then the appeal: remember what he told you in Galilee. The resurrection is no surprise to those who recall his own words; the angels point the women back to the prophecy they had forgotten.

So the witnesses hear in the angels’ word the first declaration of the risen Christ, joined to a rebuke of forgetful faith. “He is not here, but is risen”: the empty tomb is interpreted at last. The witnesses note that the angels do not merely assert the fact but recall the women to Christ’s own prediction, made plainly in Galilee — that the Son of man must die and rise the third day. They had heard it and forgotten it, their grief blotting out his promise. The verse thus teaches that the resurrection was foretold and should have been expected; the fault lay not in the evidence but in the disciples’ failure to remember and believe. The angels’ appeal to memory is the appeal of all Scripture to the doubting heart: the word was given; recall it, and believe. The risen Lord is here proclaimed, and his own foretelling vindicated.

7:
On Luke 24:7 — “Saying, The Son of man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and the third day rise again” — the witnesses mark the prophecy recalled.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Clarke reads “sinful men” as the Gentiles, the Romans, “by whom only he could be put to death” (John 19:15). Ellicott: the adjective “sinful” does not appear in the earlier reports, used in its “popular Jewish meaning… sinners of the Gentiles” (Galatians 2:15). Gill expounds “the Son of man” as the truly human Christ, “subject to the infirmities of men.”

The verse gives the substance of the foretelling the angels recall. The Son of man must be delivered to sinful men, crucified, and rise the third day — the very things now fulfilled. The whole course of the Passion and resurrection had been laid out beforehand in the Lord’s own words.

So the witnesses see the angels rehearse the prophecy that the events have now fulfilled. Each clause — the betrayal into sinful hands, the crucifixion, the third-day rising — had been spoken by Christ and is now accomplished to the letter. The witnesses note Clarke’s reading of “sinful men” as the Gentile Romans, the only ones who could execute the sentence, and the deeper truth that the Son of man gave himself into such hands by divine necessity. The verse thus shows the resurrection morning fulfilling the Passion prophecy in every part, so that the women, recalling these words, might recognise that nothing had happened by accident or defeat. What seemed the ruin of their hopes was the appointed path, foretold and now vindicated. The angels’ rehearsal turns the disciples’ sorrow into the dawning recognition that the Lord had foreseen and foretold it all — the cross and the empty tomb alike.

So the witnesses see the angels rehearse the prophecy that the events have now fulfilled. Each clause — the betrayal into sinful hands, the crucifixion, the third-day rising — had been spoken by Christ and is now accomplished to the letter. The witnesses note Clarke’s reading of “sinful men” as the Gentile Romans, the only ones who could execute the sentence. What seemed the ruin of the disciples’ hopes was the appointed path, foretold and now vindicated — the cross and the empty tomb alike foreseen by the Lord.
8:
On Luke 24:8 — “And they remembered his words” — the witnesses mark the awakening of memory.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices note the recovery. Clarke: “the simple recollection of the words of Christ becomes often a source of comfort… for his words are the words of eternal life.” Gill: the words they “had forgot… and now had their memories refreshed… and their understandings opened by the Spirit of God.” The Reformed Calvin: “though they had made little proficiency in the doctrine of Christ, still it was not lost, but was choked up, until in due time it yielded fruit.”

The verse records the turning-point in the women. At the angels’ word, the forgotten prophecy returns to mind; they remember what Christ had said. The seed of his teaching, long buried, springs at last to life.

So the witnesses dwell on the mercy of recovered memory. The women had heard the Lord foretell his death and rising, but grief had choked the word; now, prompted by the angels, they remember. The witnesses note, with Calvin, the comforting truth that Christ’s teaching, though it seemed lost on dull and forgetful hearers, was not lost but only dormant, waiting its season to bear fruit. The recollection of his words, Clarke adds, is itself a wellspring of comfort, for they are the words of eternal life. The verse thus marks the first stirring of resurrection faith in the women: not a new revelation only, but the awakening of an old one, the angels turning them back to Christ’s own promise. It teaches that the word of the Lord, hidden in the heart, may seem forgotten yet is never wasted, and that the Spirit revives it in due time to bring understanding and joy — as here, on the morning of the resurrection.

9:
On Luke 24:9 — “And returned from the sepulchre, and told all these things unto the eleven, and to all the rest” — the witnesses mark the women’s proclamation.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “told” (apengeilan) — “it was a wonderful proclamation,” Luke not separating Mary Magdalene’s story from the other women’s as John does (John 20:2). Gill: they “fled from the sepulchre in great haste… with fear and reverence… and with joy.”

The verse sends the women out as the first heralds of the resurrection. Returning from the tomb, they tell the eleven and all the rest what they have seen and heard. The news of the empty grave and the angels’ word is carried, first of all, by these faithful women.

So the witnesses honour the women as the first bearers of the gospel of the risen Christ. They came in sorrow to anoint a corpse; they return in haste, fear, and joy, to proclaim that he is risen. The witnesses note the dignity of their commission: the news the whole world would come to rest upon was first entrusted to women and first carried by them to the apostles. They mark the mingled emotions Gill describes — the awe of the vision, the gladness of the message — that sped them on their way. The verse thus shows the resurrection passing from the angels’ lips to the women’s and from the women to the eleven, the first link in the chain of witness that would reach the ends of the earth. The faithful love that brought them early to the tomb now makes them the earliest preachers of the empty grave — though, as the next verse shows, their word will not at first be believed.

10:
On Luke 24:10 — “It was Mary Magdalene, and Joanna, and Mary the mother of James, and other women that were with them, which told these things unto the apostles” — the witnesses mark the named witnesses.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify them. Ellicott: Luke “alone names” Joanna in the resurrection history, as he alone had named her before (Luke 8:3) — “not an unreasonable inference… that she was probably his chief informant.” Clarke notes Joanna was “the wife of Chuza, Herod’s steward.” Gill recalls Mary Magdalene, “out of whom Christ had cast seven devils.”

The verse names the women who bore the news. Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary the mother of James, and others — real and named witnesses, whose testimony Luke records with care. The resurrection rests not on rumour but on the report of known persons.

So the witnesses note the historical solidity that the named witnesses give. These are not anonymous figures but women known in the gospel story — Mary Magdalene, delivered of seven devils; Joanna, wife of Herod’s steward; Mary the mother of James. The witnesses observe Ellicott’s likely inference that Joanna, named by Luke alone here and earlier, was his special informant, lending the account the weight of firsthand testimony. The verse thus underscores that the resurrection is reported by particular, identifiable witnesses, whose names and histories could be checked — a mark of the gospel’s honesty. The faithful women who had followed and served Christ from Galilee are now the first credible reporters of his rising. Their testimony, carefully recorded, becomes part of the evidence on which faith in the resurrection rests — though the apostles, as the next verse shows, will at first refuse it.

11:
On Luke 24:11 — “And their words seemed to them as idle tales, and they believed them not” — the witnesses mark the apostles’ unbelief.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the striking word. Robertson: “idle talk” (leros), “old word for nonsense, only here in the N.T.… medical writers used it for the wild talk of those in delirium or hysteria”; “disbelieved” (imperfect) — “they kept on distrusting.” Ellicott: the word “is applied strictly to the trifling, half-idiotic babble of dotage.” Barnes ranges other instances of disbelieved good news (Genesis 19:14; Acts 12:9).

The verse records the cold reception of the women’s report. To the apostles the news sounded like nonsense — idle, delirious talk — and they would not believe it. The first preaching of the resurrection met with flat disbelief from those who loved Christ most.

So the witnesses mark the apostles’ unbelief as both a fault and, unexpectedly, a testimony. The women’s words struck the eleven as the raving of overwrought minds; Luke, with a physician’s precision, uses the very word for delirious babble. The witnesses note the honesty of the record: the Gospels do not portray the apostles as credulous men eager to believe a resurrection, but as hard-headed doubters who dismissed the first report. This very reluctance, they observe, strengthens the evidence — the disciples were not predisposed to believe, but had to be convinced against their will. The verse thus shows that the resurrection faith did not arise from wishful thinking but had to overcome stubborn unbelief in its first witnesses. It also rebukes the slowness of heart that would not credit the women’s true report — the same slowness the risen Lord will later reprove on the Emmaus road.

12:
On Luke 24:12 — “Then arose Peter, and ran unto the sepulchre; and stooping down, he beheld the linen clothes laid by themselves, and departed, wondering” — the witnesses mark Peter at the empty tomb.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the visit and its evidence. Clarke: John went with him and arrived first (John 20:3); the “linen clothes laid by themselves” are “no mean proof” of an orderly rising, not a stolen body. Robertson notes the whole verse is a “Western non-interpolation,” paralleled in John 20:5. The Reformed Calvin thinks Luke “inverts the order,” the running having preceded. The Eastern Eusebius marks Peter as “of more ardent feelings than the rest.”

The verse shows Peter’s response to the women’s report. Though he had not believed, he rises and runs to the tomb; stooping in, he sees the grave-clothes lying by themselves, and goes away wondering. The empty tomb and the ordered linens give him pause, though not yet full faith.

So the witnesses see in Peter’s visit the first apostolic step toward resurrection faith. Disbelieving the women, yet unable to rest, he runs to see for himself — the ardour the fathers note in him. The witnesses fasten on the detail of the linen clothes “laid by themselves”: had the body been stolen, the grave-clothes would have gone with it or been left in disorder; their neat arrangement points to a body risen, not removed. Peter departs “wondering” — not yet believing, but no longer dismissing; the evidence has begun its work. The verse thus traces the slow dawning of faith even in the chief apostle: from disbelief, to investigation, to wonder. The empty tomb that left the women perplexed and the apostles incredulous now sets Peter pondering — one more step on the road that will end, for all of them, in the joyful certainty of the risen Lord.

Peter departs “wondering” — not yet believing, but no longer dismissing; the evidence has begun its work on the chief apostle.
13:
On Luke 24:13 — “And, behold, two of them went that same day to a village called Emmaus, which was from Jerusalem about threescore furlongs” — the witnesses open the Emmaus road.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the appearance. Gregory: the Lord “refrained from manifesting to them a form which they might recognize, doing… outwardly… what was done by themselves inwardly” — “they in themselves inwardly both loved and doubted.” The expositors debate the unnamed companion: Theophylact records that “some say Luke was one of these two… and concealed his name”; Lightfoot argues for Peter (from 1 Corinthians 15:5); Cleopas is named in Luke 24:18. Robertson notes “sixty stadia… about seven miles.”

The Puritan Henry: this happened “the same day that Christ rose, the first day of the new world that rose with him.”

So the witnesses open the long and treasured narrative of Emmaus, peculiar to Luke. Two disciples, downcast, walk the seven miles from Jerusalem on the very day of the resurrection. The witnesses note the debate over the second traveller’s identity — Luke himself, by modest concealment? Peter? — while Cleopas is named. They mark Gregory’s spiritual reading: the Lord appeared in a form they could not recognise, mirroring outwardly their inward state of love mixed with doubt. The verse thus begins one of the most tender of the resurrection appearances, in which the risen Christ joins two disconsolate followers on the road, conceals himself awhile, and leads them by the Scriptures from sorrow to recognition. The setting — an ordinary journey on the first day of the new creation — prepares the lesson that the risen Lord draws near to the doubting and the grieving, walking with them unseen until their hearts are ready to know him.

14:
On Luke 24:14 — “And they talked together of all these things which had happened” — the witnesses mark the disciples’ sorrowful conversation.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “they communed” (homiloun), from which “our word homiletics is derived,” preaching having been at first “largely conversational.” The Reformed Calvin reads their godliness amid grief: “it was a proof of godliness that they endeavored to cherish their faith in Christ, though small and weak… as a shield against the offense of the cross.”

The verse shows the two disciples engrossed in talk of the recent events. As they walk, they speak together of all that has happened — the cross, the empty tomb, the women’s report — turning over in sorrow the things that have shaken their hopes.

So the witnesses find in the disciples’ conversation a mingling of grief and lingering faith. They cannot let the matter go; they talk it over as they walk, their minds full of the crucified Master and the strange reports of the morning. The witnesses note, with Calvin, the godliness in this very discourse: though their faith was small and confused, they clung to it, seeking in their reverence for Christ a shield against the scandal of his cross. They did not abandon him in their disappointment but kept him the subject of their thoughts and words. The verse thus shows the right use of holy conversation in a time of doubt — the troubled disciples reasoning together about the Lord — and prepares the gracious sequel: that to such as are occupied with him, even in perplexity, the risen Christ draws near. Their sorrowful talk becomes the occasion of his coming.

15:
On Luke 24:15 — “And it came to pass, that, while they communed together and reasoned, Jesus himself drew near, and went with them” — the witnesses mark the risen Lord joining them.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the moment. Barnes: “the disciples were properly employed… their minds were anxious about the state of things,” when Jesus drew near. Robertson: “Jesus himself” (autos Iesous), “in actual person… was going along with them.” Clarke notes the “laudable custom” of conversing about the law in journeys. Ellicott: neither traveller belonged to the Twelve, yet the Lord chose to manifest himself to them.

The verse brings the risen Christ unrecognised into the disciples’ company. As they talk and reason of him, he himself draws near and walks with them — the very Lord they mourn, present in person, though they do not know it.

So the witnesses dwell on the grace of the Lord’s drawing near. The two disciples were speaking of Christ; and Christ, in person, joined them on the road. The witnesses note the tender providence in it: they were properly and devoutly employed, reasoning of their Master, and to such he came. The Lord does not wait to be sought but draws near of his own accord to the grieving and perplexed who are occupied with him. The witnesses mark that, though risen and glorified, he condescends to walk a country road with two obscure disciples, neither of them apostles. The verse thus enacts the abiding truth that the risen Christ companies with his own, often unrecognised, in the ordinary ways of life — especially when they speak and think of him. Their unwitting walk with the living Lord, while they mourn him as dead, is the gentle irony that frames the whole Emmaus story, and the promise that he is nearer to his sorrowing people than they know.

16:
On Luke 24:16 — “But their eyes were holden that they should not know him” — the witnesses weigh why the disciples did not recognise the Lord.

The grammatical-historical commentators incline to a natural cause. Barnes and Clarke: there was nothing “supernatural or miraculous,” for Jesus “appeared in another form” (Mark 16:12) — different from his familiar look. Robertson: “continued being held… from recognizing him fully.” The Reformed Calvin guards the truth of the body: the evangelist states it “lest any one should think that the aspect of Christ’s body was changed”; the Lord remained himself, “but was not recognized, because the eyes of beholders were held.”

The verse explains the disciples’ blindness. Their eyes were held, that they should not know him. Whether by a change in his appearance or a restraint on their sight, they were kept, for a time, from recognising the Lord who walked beside them.

So the witnesses consider the holding of the disciples’ eyes and divide gently on its cause. Some, like Barnes and Clarke, find it natural — the risen Lord appeared “in another form,” and grief and unexpectedness kept them from knowing him. Others, with Calvin, see a real restraint laid on their perception, lest they think his body changed; he was truly himself, but their eyes were held until the appointed moment. Either way, the witnesses agree on the purpose: the Lord meant to instruct them from the Scriptures before revealing himself, that their faith might rest not on sight alone but on the word. The verse thus shows the divine pedagogy of the Emmaus walk: the Lord conceals himself awhile, not to deceive but to teach, holding their eyes until their hearts have been prepared by his opening of the Scriptures — so that the recognition, when it comes, will be the fruit of faith and not merely of sight.

So the witnesses consider the holding of the disciples’ eyes and divide gently on its cause. Some find it natural — the risen Lord appeared “in another form,” and grief kept them from knowing him. Others, with Calvin, see a real restraint laid on their perception, lest they think his body changed. Either way, the witnesses agree on the purpose: the Lord meant to instruct them from the Scriptures before revealing himself, holding their eyes until their hearts were prepared — that the recognition, when it came, would be the fruit of faith and not merely of sight.
17:
On Luke 24:17 — “And he said unto them, What manner of communications are these that ye have one to another, as ye walk, and are sad?” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s drawing-out question.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the vivid Greek. Robertson: “that ye have with another” (antiballete, to throw back and forth like a ball) is “a beautiful picture of conversation as a game of words”; the better text adds “and they stood still, looking sad” (Ellicott). Barnes: “they were deeply affected… and were conversing about him, and were sad.” The Reformed Calvin: what was “done openly by Christ… we daily feel… that of his own accord he approaches us unperceived for the purpose of instructing us.”

The verse gives the Lord’s gentle opening. He asks what they are discussing so earnestly, and why they are sad — drawing out their grief that he may answer it. The question stops them in their tracks, their downcast faces betraying their sorrow.

So the witnesses see the risen Lord begin his ministry to the two by inviting them to unburden their hearts. He knows their grief, yet asks of it, that they may speak it and so be ready to be taught. The witnesses note the vivid picture of their conversation — words tossed back and forth like a ball — and the arresting effect of his question, which halts them, sad-faced, on the road. They mark Calvin’s application: that the Lord still draws near unperceived to instruct his people, often through the very questions that lead them to voice their trouble. The verse thus shows the wisdom of the divine Teacher, who does not at once correct but first listens, drawing out the disciples’ sorrow and confusion so that he may meet it from the Scriptures. Their sadness, owned aloud, becomes the doorway through which he will lead them to understanding and joy.

So the witnesses see the risen Lord begin his ministry to the two by inviting them to unburden their hearts. He knows their grief, yet asks of it, that they may speak it and so be ready to be taught. The witnesses note the vivid picture of their conversation — words tossed back and forth like a ball — and the arresting effect of his question, which halts them, sad-faced, on the road. The divine Teacher does not at once correct but first listens, drawing out their sorrow so that he may meet it from the Scriptures.
18:
On Luke 24:18 — “And one of them, whose name was Cleopas, answering said unto him, Art thou only a stranger in Jerusalem, and hast not known the things which are come to pass there in these days?” — the witnesses mark Cleopas’ reply.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the name and the surprise. Ellicott distinguishes “Cleopas” (a Greek contraction of Cleopatros) from the “Clopas” of John 19:25. Robertson glosses “dost thou alone sojourn?” (paroikeis, to dwell as a stranger, Ephesians 2:19). Barnes: it is “an expression of surprise that he should be unacquainted with an affair that had made so much noise.”

The verse gives Cleopas’ astonished question. Can this fellow-traveller be the only visitor in Jerusalem who has not heard of the things lately done there? The events of the Passion had been so public that ignorance of them seems incredible.

So the witnesses note the irony that fills the exchange. Cleopas marvels that the stranger should not know what happened in Jerusalem — when the stranger is the very centre of those events, the One to whom they all pointed. The witnesses observe the unconscious truth in his words: the things “come to pass” were indeed the talk of the city, the death and reported rising of Jesus; yet the one he supposes ignorant knows them better than any, having endured them. They mark the surprise as a measure of how notorious the crucifixion had been — no casual sojourner could have missed it. The verse thus deepens the gentle irony of the Emmaus story: the risen Lord, asking after his own death as though a stranger to it, draws from the disciples the very confession of their grief and bewildered hope — that he may answer, from the Scriptures, the events they think he has not heard.

So the witnesses note the irony that fills the exchange. Cleopas marvels that the stranger should not know what happened in Jerusalem — when the stranger is the very centre of those events. The witnesses observe the unconscious truth in his words: the things “come to pass” were indeed the talk of the city, yet the one he supposes ignorant knows them better than any, having endured them. The risen Lord, asking after his own death as though a stranger to it, draws from the disciples the very confession of their grief — that he may answer it from the Scriptures.
19:
On Luke 24:19 — “And he said unto them, What things? And they said unto him, Concerning Jesus of Nazareth, which was a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people” — the witnesses mark the disciples’ confession of faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators measure the level of their belief. Ellicott: “they believed in Jesus as a prophet; they hoped that He would redeem Israel… they had not risen to the belief that He was the Christ, the Son of God.” Barnes: “mighty in deed” in miracles, “mighty in word” in teaching. Gill notes the Lord asks “What things?” “as if… ignorant… to draw out of them a particular relation.”

The verse gives the disciples’ account of Jesus. He was, they say, a prophet mighty in deed and word, esteemed before God and the people. Their estimate is high but incomplete — a great prophet, not yet confessed as the risen Christ and Son of God.

So the witnesses note the disciples’ faith at its halting stage. Drawn out by the Lord’s feigned ignorance, they pour out their view of Jesus — a prophet mighty in works and words, honoured by God and men. The witnesses mark, with Ellicott, the precise measure of their belief: real but limited; they had owned him a prophet and hoped he would redeem Israel, but the cross had shaken their fuller hope, and they had not grasped that he was the Christ risen from the dead. Their words are true as far as they go, yet fall short of the resurrection faith the Lord is about to instill. The verse thus lays bare the disciples’ need: a faith that honoured Jesus as a prophet but stumbled at his suffering and could not yet believe his rising. It is this faith, partial and wounded, that the risen Christ will complete by opening to them the Scriptures.

So the witnesses note the disciples’ faith at its halting stage. Drawn out by the Lord’s feigned ignorance, they pour out their view of Jesus — a prophet mighty in works and words, honoured by God and men. The witnesses mark, with Ellicott, the precise measure of their belief: real but limited; they had owned him a prophet and hoped he would redeem Israel, but the cross had shaken their fuller hope, and they had not grasped that he was the Christ risen. It is this faith, partial and wounded, that the risen Christ will complete by opening the Scriptures.
20:
On Luke 24:20 — “And how the chief priests and our rulers delivered him to be condemned to death, and have crucified him” — the witnesses mark the disciples’ account of the crucifixion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the precision. Ellicott: “delivered him to a sentence of death… The Sanhedrin had not, strictly speaking, passed a sentence of death… for that they had to deliver Him up to… Pilate.” Barnes points to the rulers’ act (Acts 13:27). Gill names “the chief priests and our rulers, civil and ecclesiastic.”

The verse continues the disciples’ sorrowful narrative. Their own rulers, they relate, delivered Jesus to be condemned and crucified — the very leaders of the nation handing over the prophet they had honoured. The cross is the wound at the centre of their grief.

So the witnesses hear in the disciples’ words the bitterness of the crucifixion as they understood it. The chief priests and rulers — those who should have led the people to the Christ — had instead delivered him to death. The witnesses note Ellicott’s legal nicety: the Sanhedrin condemned him on a capital charge but could not execute him, so handed him to Pilate, the crucifixion being a Roman act done at Jewish instigation. The disciples relate the fact plainly, but with the heaviness of dashed hope: the One they trusted had been put to a shameful death by their own nation’s authorities. The verse thus voices the offence of the cross that lay on the disciples’ hearts — the stumbling-block the risen Lord has come to remove. It is precisely this death, which seemed to them the ruin of all, that he will show, from the Scriptures, to have been the appointed and necessary path to the Messiah’s glory.

So the witnesses hear in the disciples’ words the bitterness of the crucifixion as they understood it. The chief priests and rulers — those who should have led the people to the Christ — had instead delivered him to death. The witnesses note Ellicott’s legal nicety: the Sanhedrin condemned him on a capital charge but handed him to Pilate, the crucifixion being a Roman act at Jewish instigation. The verse voices the offence of the cross that lay on the disciples’ hearts — the stumbling-block the risen Lord has come to remove and show to be the appointed path to glory.
21:
On Luke 24:21 — “But we trusted that it had been he which should have redeemed Israel: and beside all this, to day is the third day since these things were done” — the witnesses mark the disciples’ disappointed hope.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic hope and its nature. Robertson: “but we hoped” (imperfect), with emphasis on “we”; “redeem” from the bondage of Rome. Lightfoot: they hoped for redemption “from the Gentile yoke,” the national dream. Barnes notes Cleopas’ confused, disjointed speech — “his mind is confused and distracted.” The Reformed Calvin: their hope “was not broken off, though… such might appear to be the import of their words.”

The verse lays bare the disciples’ wounded hope. They had trusted Jesus would redeem Israel; his death seemed to end that hope. And now it is the third day — the very point at which his promise should be fulfilled, yet they speak as men whose expectation has failed.

So the witnesses hear the heart of the disciples’ sorrow: “we trusted… but.” Their hope had been real but largely earthly — that Jesus would redeem Israel from the Roman yoke — and the cross had shattered it. The witnesses note the confusion in Cleopas’ broken speech, the mingling of fading hope with the strange reports of the morning, and the poignant mention of “the third day,” the very day the Lord had named for his rising, though they do not grasp its significance. They mark Calvin’s observation that their hope was not wholly extinguished but choked and bewildered. The verse thus shows faith at its lowest ebb on the resurrection day itself — disappointed, confused, mourning a redemption it thought lost — the very condition the risen Lord, walking beside them, has come to heal. Their “we trusted” is the sigh of every disciple whose hopes the cross has shaken, awaiting the word that will raise them.

So the witnesses hear the heart of the disciples’ sorrow: “we trusted… but.” Their hope had been real but largely earthly — that Jesus would redeem Israel from the Roman yoke — and the cross had shattered it. The witnesses note the confusion in Cleopas’ broken speech, and the poignant mention of “the third day,” the very day the Lord had named for his rising, though they do not grasp its significance. They mark Calvin’s observation that their hope was not wholly extinguished but choked — the very condition the risen Lord, walking beside them, has come to heal.
22:
On Luke 24:22 — “Yea, and certain women also of our company made us astonished, which were early at the sepulchre” — the witnesses mark the report of the women.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators note the language. Robertson: “amazed us” (exestesan, the root of “ecstasy”) — “they startled us”; “early” at daybreak. Ellicott: “literally, they startled us.” Gill notes the women were “of our company… who came with them from Galilee.”

The verse adds the women’s report to the disciples’ account. The women of their company, early at the tomb, had astonished them with what they found and heard. Into the disciples’ grief comes this startling, half-credited news.

So the witnesses note that the disciples already knew the women’s report, yet it had not turned their sorrow to joy. The news startled them — the very word, Robertson observes, is the root of “ecstasy” — but astonishment is not faith. The witnesses mark the disciples’ state: they had heard of the empty tomb and the angels, and were amazed, but they walked still in sadness, the report sitting uneasily alongside their disappointed hope. They could neither dismiss it nor believe it. The verse thus shows the women’s testimony reaching the Emmaus travellers without yet convincing them — one more witness to the resurrection that fell on doubting ears. It prepares the contrast the Lord will draw: that the things which astonished them, rightly understood from the Scriptures, were not cause for bewilderment but the very fulfilment of prophecy, the ground not of confusion but of faith.

So the witnesses note that the disciples already knew the women’s report, yet it had not turned their sorrow to joy. The news startled them — the very word, Robertson observes, is the root of “ecstasy” — but astonishment is not faith. They could neither dismiss it nor believe it — one more witness to the resurrection that fell on doubting ears.
23:
On Luke 24:23 — “And when they found not his body, they came, saying, that they had also seen a vision of angels, which said that he was alive” — the witnesses mark the angelic message reported.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Ellicott: “vision” is used of what Zacharias saw in the Temple (Luke 1:22); the word “does not occur elsewhere” in this sense. Robertson: “all this was too indirect and uncertain (women and angels) for Cleopas and his companion.”

The verse completes the women’s report as the disciples relate it. The women found no body, and told of a vision of angels declaring that Jesus was alive. The heart of the resurrection message is here — “he is alive” — yet it reaches the disciples as mere report.

So the witnesses see the disciples recount the very gospel without yet believing it. The women had found the tomb empty and brought word, from angels, that the Lord lived; but to the Emmaus travellers this was “too indirect and uncertain,” as Robertson notes — women’s testimony, angelic visions, no sight of the living Christ himself. The witnesses mark the tragedy and the irony: they repeat the announcement “he is alive” to the living Lord who walks beside them, and still do not believe. The message had been delivered, the evidence given, yet faith faltered for want of seeing. The verse thus shows how near the disciples were to the truth and how far — possessing the report of the resurrection, even the angels’ “he is alive,” yet sunk in sadness. It is this gap between report and faith that the risen Lord will close, not by mere appearance, but by opening the Scriptures that foretold it all.

So the witnesses see the disciples recount the very gospel without yet believing it. The women had found the tomb empty and brought word, from angels, that the Lord lived; but to the Emmaus travellers this was “too indirect and uncertain.” The witnesses mark the irony: they repeat the announcement “he is alive” to the living Lord who walks beside them, and still do not believe. The message had been delivered, the evidence given, yet faith faltered for want of seeing — the gap the risen Lord will close by opening the Scriptures that foretold it all.
24:
On Luke 24:24 — “And certain of them which were with us went to the sepulchre, and found it even so as the women had said: but him they saw not” — the witnesses mark the apostles’ confirmation.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the visit. Barnes: “certain of them” are Peter and John (John 20:3). Ellicott notes the “obviously undesigned coincidence” with John’s report — the natural way the two apostles “are mentioned, but not named” — a mark of the narrative’s genuineness. Gill: the apostles found the tomb “even so” as the women said.

The verse adds the apostles’ corroboration to the women’s report. Some of the disciples went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said — empty — but they did not see Jesus. The empty tomb is confirmed; the living Lord, to them, is not yet seen.

So the witnesses note the mounting evidence that still fell short of faith for the Emmaus two. The women’s report was confirmed by the apostles’ own visit: the tomb was empty, as they had said. The witnesses mark Ellicott’s point that the casual, unnamed mention of “certain of them” — Peter and John — coincides undesignedly with John’s fuller account, a quiet sign of truthfulness. Yet the corroboration ended with the empty tomb: “him they saw not.” The disciples had the fact of the absent body, attested now by men as well as women, but lacked the sight of the risen Christ that would compel belief. The verse thus sets the stage for the Lord’s rebuke and instruction: the evidence of the empty tomb, the women’s word, the angels’ message, and the apostles’ confirmation all lay before them, yet they remained sad and slow of heart — needing not more facts but the Scriptures opened, that they might believe.

So the witnesses note the mounting evidence that still fell short of faith for the Emmaus two. The women’s report was confirmed by the apostles’ own visit: the tomb was empty, as they had said. The witnesses mark Ellicott’s point that the casual, unnamed mention of “certain of them” — Peter and John — coincides undesignedly with John’s account, a quiet sign of truthfulness. Yet the corroboration ended with the empty tomb: “him they saw not.” They needed not more facts but the Scriptures opened, that they might believe.
25:
On Luke 24:25 — “Then he said unto them, O fools, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s reproof.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the rebuke and its purpose. Theophylact: the Lord reproves their “too much doubt”; they were “slow of heart to believe all… the prophets” — for “it is possible to believe some” and not all. Gregory and Augustine read the Lord still “feigning” to go further, accompanying them as he expounds. The grammatical-historical commentators soften the word: Robertson, “without sense” (anoetoi), not the harsher term of Matthew 5:22; Ellicott notes it is Paul’s word for the “foolish Galatians” (Galatians 3:1).

The verse gives the Lord’s gentle yet pointed rebuke. He calls them senseless and slow of heart — not for ignorance of facts, but for failing to believe the prophets, who had foretold all that troubled them. Their sadness sprang from unbelief of Scripture.

So the witnesses see the risen Lord diagnose the disciples’ true ailment. Their grief came not from want of evidence — they had the empty tomb, the angels’ word, the apostles’ report — but from a heart slow to believe what the prophets had plainly spoken. The witnesses note that the word for “fools” is not contemptuous but means “without understanding,” a rebuke of dulness, not a curse. Calvin observes that the reproof, sharp as it seems, was just, for the Lord had long labored with them to little fruit. The fault lay in believing only part of the prophets — the glory, not the suffering. The verse thus turns the disciples from their confused sorrow to the root of their unbelief and to its remedy: the Scriptures, which had foretold both the Messiah’s death and his rising. The Lord’s rebuke is the prelude to his great exposition, leading them from the prophets to faith in the risen Christ.

So the witnesses see the risen Lord diagnose the disciples’ true ailment. Their grief came not from want of evidence but from a heart slow to believe what the prophets had plainly spoken. The witnesses note that the word for “fools” is not contemptuous but means “without understanding,” a rebuke of dulness, not a curse. Calvin observes that the reproof, sharp as it seems, was just. The fault lay in believing only part of the prophets — the glory, not the suffering — and the Lord’s rebuke is the prelude to his great exposition.
26:
On Luke 24:26 — “Ought not Christ to have suffered these things, and to enter into his glory?” — the witnesses mark the necessity of the suffering Messiah.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the divine necessity. Robertson: “was it not necessary? The very things about the death of Jesus that disturbed them… were the strongest proof that he was the Messiah.” Ellicott: the thought that the sufferings were “a necessary condition of the glory” became “almost an axiom of Christian thought” (1 Peter 1:11). Clarke: “this was the way in which sin must be expiated.” Wesley: “these very sufferings which occasion your doubts are the proofs of his being the Messiah.”

The verse gives the heart of the Lord’s teaching. The Christ must suffer and so enter his glory; the cross was no defeat but the appointed road to the crown. The very thing that shook the disciples’ faith was the proof of the Messiah they sought.

So the witnesses find in this question the great correction of the disciples’ error. They had stumbled at the cross, thinking it the ruin of their hopes; the Lord shows it was a divine necessity, foretold by the prophets — the suffering the appointed path to the glory. The witnesses note that the order is fixed: first the suffering, then the glory (1 Peter 1:11); the Messiah must be wounded before he is crowned. Clarke marks the deeper reason: only by this suffering could sin be expiated and the world saved. The very sufferings that occasioned the disciples’ doubt were, rightly understood, the strongest proof of the Christ. The verse thus overturns the disciples’ whole misreading: the cross was not the contradiction of the Messiah’s mission but its fulfilment, the necessary gateway to his glory and the resurrection they could not yet believe. The Lord teaches them to read suffering and glory together, as the prophets had joined them.

So the witnesses find in this question the great correction of the disciples’ error. They had stumbled at the cross, thinking it the ruin of their hopes; the Lord shows it was a divine necessity, foretold by the prophets — the suffering the appointed path to the glory. The witnesses note that the order is fixed: first the suffering, then the glory (1 Pet 1:11). Clarke marks the deeper reason: only by this suffering could sin be expiated. The cross was not the contradiction of the Messiah’s mission but its fulfilment, the necessary gateway to his glory.
27:
On Luke 24:27 — “And beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself” — the witnesses mark the great exposition.

The grammatical-historical commentators marvel at the discourse. Clarke: “what a sermon this must have been, where all the prophecies… were all adduced, illustrated, and applied to himself.” Robertson: “interpreted” (diermeneusen), the verb from which we get “hermeneutics.” Ellicott: “here… a full opening of the Scriptures on all that pertained to… the Christ.” The Reformed Calvin: this “shows us in what manner Christ is made known… when light is thrown on the knowledge of him by the Law and the Prophets.”

The verse gives the climax of the Emmaus walk. Beginning at Moses and going through all the prophets, the Lord expounds to them everything in the Scriptures concerning himself. The whole Old Testament is opened as a witness to the suffering and glorified Christ.

So the witnesses stand in wonder at the sermon none recorded but all revered. The risen Lord traces, from Genesis through the prophets, the testimony of all Scripture to himself — his suffering, death, and rising foretold in type and prophecy. The witnesses note that this is the divine pattern of all true teaching: Christ made known through the Law and the Prophets, the Old Testament read as a witness to him. Gill instances the first promise of the bruised heel, the types of Isaac and the brazen serpent; Calvin marks that the Lord himself is the best expositor, founding the gospel on the proof of the older Scriptures. The verse thus reveals the key to the whole Bible — that Moses and all the prophets speak of Christ — and shows the risen Lord teaching his disciples to find him there. It is by this opening of the Scriptures, more than by sight, that their hearts begin to burn and their faith to revive, preparing the recognition that will come at the breaking of bread.

The risen Lord traces, from Genesis through the prophets, the testimony of all Scripture to himself — his suffering, death, and rising foretold in type and prophecy. The witnesses note that this is the divine pattern of all true teaching: Christ made known through the Law and the Prophets. Calvin marks that the Lord himself is the best expositor. 28:
"And they drew nigh unto the village, whither they went: and he made as though he would have gone further." The witnesses note Christ's seeming intention to go on, and its purpose. Barnes explains: "he did not say he would go farther, but he kept on as if it was not his intention to stop, and doubtless he would have gone on if they had not constrained him to tarry." Clarke draws the lesson: his preaching "had made a deep impression upon their hearts (Luke 24:32), and now they feel it their greatest privilege to entertain the preacher... wherever [the doctrine of Christ] is felt, the ever-blessed Jesus is earnestly entreated to dwell in the heart." The witnesses note Christ acting as though he would pass on, drawing out the disciples' desire.

The witnesses note Christ's seeming purpose to go further — testing and drawing out the disciples' longing for him.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's seeming intention to go on. The lesson the expositors draw is the way of Christ in drawing out the desires of his people, that they might earnestly seek his abiding presence. The witnesses note that as they neared Emmaus, Christ "made as though he would have gone further" — not by any word of deceit, but by his manner, keeping on as if he meant to travel further; and (Barnes notes) he doubtless would have gone on had they not constrained him to stay. The expositors observe the purpose in this: Christ tests and draws out the disciples' desire for his company; by seeming to depart, he draws from them the earnest entreaty to abide — as he had drawn out the Canaanite woman's faith by seeming to refuse (Matthew 15:23-28), so here he draws out the disciples' longing by seeming to leave. The witnesses note the example this offers of how Christ deals with his people: he often withdraws, or seems to, that he may be the more earnestly sought; the apparent departures of Christ are meant to quicken desire and to make his presence the more prized. The expositors draw the lesson Clarke names: Christ's discourse had warmed their hearts, and now they count it their greatest privilege to entertain the preacher; this is the constant effect of the doctrine of Christ — wherever it is truly felt, the heart earnestly entreats the Author of it to dwell within; the soul that has tasted his word will not let him go. The witnesses press the responsibility of the disciples: Christ would have gone on had they not pressed him to stay; so the enjoyment of his presence depends, under grace, on the earnest seeking of it — he abides with those who constrain him, who will not be content to let him depart. The expositors observe the tenderness of Christ's method: he does not force his company on the indifferent, but rewards the earnest desire of those who long for him; the seeming departure the means of the deeper communion that follows. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to seek Christ's abiding presence earnestly, not letting him seem to pass on; to constrain him by prayer and longing, that he may stay and reveal himself. The verse thus records Christ's seeming purpose to go further — drawing out the disciples' desire — teaching the way of Christ in quickening the longing of his people, and the privilege of entreating him to abide.
29:
"But they constrained him, saying, Abide with us: for it is toward evening, and the day is far spent. And he went in to tarry with them." The witnesses note the disciples' earnest entreaty. Gill clarifies "constrained" — not force, but "very urgent and importunate... they would take no denial"; the Persic version, "with importunity said unto him." Barnes notes "they had been charmed and delighted with his discourse, and they wished to hear him farther; Christians are delighted with communion with the Saviour... and find their chief pleasure in fellowship with him." The witnesses note the disciples pressing Christ to stay, and his going in to abide with them.

The witnesses note the disciples' importunate plea — "Abide with us" — and Christ's gracious consent to stay.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' earnest entreaty and Christ's consent. The lesson the expositors draw is the importunity that constrains Christ to abide, and the delight of his people in his fellowship. The witnesses note the plea: "Abide with us: for it is toward evening, and the day is far spent" — the disciples urge Christ to stay, pressing the lateness of the hour as their reason, but moved (the expositors note) by the deeper longing for his company. Gill clarifies the word "constrained": not that they laid hold on him by force, but that they were urgent, importunate, pressing — they "would take no denial," entreating him with a holy violence to stay. The expositors observe the delight that prompted the plea, which Barnes names: the disciples had been charmed and delighted by Christ's discourse, their hearts burning within them, and they longed to hear him further; the believer's chief pleasure is fellowship with the Saviour, and they could not bear to let him go. The witnesses press the lesson on importunate seeking: Christ rewards the earnest, pressing desire of his people; he who seemed about to depart yields to their importunity and goes in to abide — the holy violence of longing prevailing with him (as in Genesis 32:26, "I will not let thee go except thou bless me"). The expositors draw out the encouragement: the believer who will not let Christ go, who constrains him by earnest prayer and desire, finds him willing to stay and reveal himself; the abiding presence is granted to the importunate. The witnesses note the gracious consent: "he went in to tarry with them" — Christ yields, enters the house, and stays; the reward of their entreaty the deeper communion and the revelation that follows (the breaking of bread). The expositors observe the application to the evening of life and the close of day: as the disciples entreated Christ at evening, so the believer may pray, "Abide with me, fast falls the eventide" — seeking his presence especially as the day and life decline. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to constrain Christ by importunate desire, to delight in his fellowship, and to entreat him to abide; for he stays with those who will not let him go. The verse thus records the disciples' entreaty and Christ's consent — Abide with us — teaching the importunity that constrains Christ, the delight of his people in his fellowship, and the abiding presence granted to earnest desire.
30:
"And it came to pass, as he sat at meat with them, he took bread, and blessed it, and brake, and gave to them." The witnesses note Christ's familiar action and a question whether it was the Eucharist. Lightfoot argues it was not the holy Supper but the ordinary blessing of a meal: "it is strange that any should expound this breaking of bread of the holy eucharist, when Christ had determined to disappear in the very distribution of the bread, and so interrupt the supper... where indeed doth it appear that any of them tasted a bit? The supper was ended before it began" — Christ using some ordinary form of blessing, "not the words, This is my body." The witnesses note Christ taking the place of host, blessing and breaking the bread in his familiar manner.

The witnesses note Christ's familiar act of blessing and breaking bread — the ordinary grace by which they knew him.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's familiar action at the table. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ revealed himself in the ordinary act of blessing and breaking bread, and that this was not the Eucharist but the common grace of a meal. The witnesses note that Christ, though the guest, "took bread, and blessed it, and brake, and gave to them" — assuming the place of the host, the head of the table, blessing and breaking the bread as he had so often done; the familiar manner of his action being, it seems, the occasion of their recognizing him. The expositors weigh the question whether this was the Lord's Supper, and Lightfoot argues strongly that it was not: Christ vanished in the very act of distributing the bread, interrupting and ending the meal before it began, so that none of them is said to have eaten; this was no celebration of the Eucharist, but the ordinary blessing of a meal, with some common form of grace, not the words of institution. The witnesses note the force of the argument: the breaking of bread here is the customary act of a Jewish host giving thanks and dividing the bread at the start of a meal, not the sacrament; and the disciples knew Christ not by any sacramental sign but by the familiar manner of his blessing and breaking. The expositors draw the lesson on the revealing of Christ in the ordinary: it was in a common act, the blessing of daily bread, that Christ made himself known — teaching that he reveals himself not only in extraordinary signs but in the ordinary means and moments, the familiar acts of grace and fellowship. The witnesses observe the providence: perhaps the manner of his taking, blessing, and breaking — so often seen, perhaps marked by the prints in his hands — stirred their memory and opened their eyes (the next verse). The expositors note that the recognition came at the table, in the act of his hospitality and grace — the guest become the host, the stranger revealed as the Lord in the breaking of bread. The witnesses draw the application: the believer may find Christ revealed in the ordinary acts of grace and the common fellowship of the table, and is to look for him there. The verse thus records Christ's familiar act of blessing and breaking bread — teaching the revealing of Christ in the ordinary, and (with Lightfoot) that this was the common grace of a meal, not the Eucharist, the occasion of their knowing him.
31:
"And their eyes were opened, and they knew him; and he vanished out of their sight." The witnesses note the moment of recognition and Christ's disappearance. Gill explains "their eyes were opened" — not that they were blind before, but "what before held them was removed... what hindered their sight and knowledge was taken away," perhaps the familiar actions stirring their memory. Barnes notes "the obscurity was removed; they saw him to be the Messiah... their doubts were gone." On the vanishing, Barnes notes "he suddenly departed... during their surprise, he took the opportunity suddenly to withdraw." The witnesses note the disciples knowing Christ at last, and his immediate vanishing.

The witnesses note the opening of their eyes — they knew the risen Christ — and his sudden vanishing.

So the witnesses make the verse the recognition of the risen Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the sovereign opening of the eyes by which Christ is known, and the purpose of his sudden withdrawal. The witnesses note the moment: "their eyes were opened, and they knew him" — the obscurity removed, the hindrance to their sight taken away; and they recognized the stranger as the risen Jesus, their doubts gone, their hope confirmed. The expositors press Gill's careful explanation: their eyes had not been blind, but something had held them, kept them from knowing him (v.16) — whether a divine restraint or their own unbelief and grief; and now that restraint was removed, perhaps the familiar manner of his blessing and breaking stirring their memory, and they saw who he was. The witnesses observe the sovereignty in the opening: it was God who held their eyes and God who opened them, in his own time and way; the recognition of the risen Christ a gift, not a mere act of natural sight — they knew him when their eyes were opened, not before. The expositors draw the lesson on the spiritual parallel: as Christ opened the disciples' eyes to know him, so he opens the eyes of the soul to behold him in his glory; none knows Christ savingly but by the opening of the eyes that God alone can give. The witnesses note the sudden vanishing: "he vanished out of their sight" — at the very moment of recognition, Christ withdrew, suddenly departing (Barnes notes, taking the opportunity of their surprise to withdraw). The expositors weigh the purpose of this withdrawal: Christ revealed himself only to vanish, that the disciples might learn to live by faith, not sight; the brief revelation enough to assure them of his resurrection, but the vanishing teaching them that his bodily presence was now to be withdrawn, and they were to walk by faith in the unseen risen Lord. The witnesses press the lesson: the glimpses of Christ granted his people in this life are brief and precious; he reveals himself, then withdraws, that faith and longing may be exercised, and that they may seek the fuller vision to come. The expositors observe the assurance the moment gave: the disciples now knew, beyond doubt, that Christ was risen — they had seen and known him; the vanishing did not undo the certainty, but sealed it. The verse thus records the recognition and vanishing of Christ — their eyes opened, then he vanished — teaching the sovereign opening of the eyes by which Christ is known, and the withdrawal that teaches his people to live by faith in the risen Lord.
32:
"And they said one to another, Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?" The witnesses note the burning heart under Christ's opening of the Scriptures. Lightfoot discusses a textual variant ("was not our heart hid/veiled?") but prefers the received sense, comparing the burning of the heart to Psalm 39:3 ("my heart was hot within me... the fire burned") and Jeremiah 20:9 ("his word was in mine heart as a burning fire"); their hearts "so affected, and grew so warm, that they could hold no longer." The witnesses note the disciples recalling how their hearts had burned as the risen Christ expounded the Scriptures to them.

The witnesses note the burning heart — the disciples' hearts kindled as Christ opened the Scriptures to them.

So the witnesses make the verse the burning heart under the word. The lesson the expositors draw is the kindling power of Christ's opening of the Scriptures, the inward witness that accompanies his word. The witnesses note the disciples' reflection, after Christ had vanished: "Did not our heart burn within us, while he talked with us by the way, and while he opened to us the scriptures?" — recalling how, even before they knew him, their hearts had glowed and burned under his exposition of the word; a warmth, a fervour, an inward fire they could not account for at the time. The expositors observe the meaning of the burning heart, which Lightfoot illustrates: their hearts were so affected, so warmed by Christ's word, that they glowed within them, as the psalmist's heart was "hot within" him while he mused (Psalm 39:3), and as Jeremiah found God's word "as a burning fire shut up in his bones" (Jeremiah 20:9); the kindling of holy affection and joy under the opening of the Scriptures. The witnesses press the source of the burning: it was Christ's opening of the Scriptures — showing how Moses and the prophets testified of him (v.27) — that kindled their hearts; the word of Christ, rightly opened, sets the heart aflame. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the opened word: when Christ, by his Spirit, opens the Scriptures, the heart burns — with conviction, with love, with joy; the inward warmth a token of his presence and a fruit of his word. The witnesses note the inward witness this gave: though their eyes were holden and they knew him not, their hearts bore witness, burning under his word — an evidence of his presence felt before it was seen; so the believer often feels the power of Christ in the word before he fully discerns him. The expositors observe the example for preaching and hearing: the word of Christ, opened and applied, should kindle the heart; the cold heart that feels no warmth under the Scriptures lacks the fire that Christ's word kindles in the believing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to seek the burning heart under the opened word, to know Christ in the warmth his Scriptures kindle, and to mark the inward witness of his presence in the fervour of holy affection. The verse thus records the burning heart — kindled as Christ opened the Scriptures — teaching the kindling power of the opened word, the inward witness of Christ's presence, and the holy fire that his Scriptures light in the believing heart.
33:
"And they rose up the same hour, and returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, and them that were with them," The witnesses note the disciples' eager haste to share the news. Gill notes that though it was evening, the day spent, and they were wearied with their journey, "being big with this appearance of Christ, and in haste to impart the joyful news... they immediately rose up." Barnes draws the lesson: "such was their joy that they hastened to tell it... it shows how quick and ready they who have found the Saviour are to tell it to others" (cf. John 1:41-45). The witnesses note the two disciples returning at once to Jerusalem to find the assembled disciples.

The witnesses note the disciples' eager haste — rising at once to carry the joyful news to the others.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' eager return. The lesson the expositors draw is the joy that cannot be contained, hastening to share the good news of the risen Christ. The witnesses note the haste: "they rose up the same hour, and returned to Jerusalem" — though it was evening, the day far spent, and they had walked the sixty furlongs from Jerusalem and were wearied, yet (Gill notes) being full of the joy of Christ's appearance, they rose at once and hastened back the long road to tell the others. The expositors press the lesson Barnes draws: such was their joy that they could not keep it, but hastened to share it — showing how quick and ready those who have found the Saviour are to tell others; the joy of the risen Christ overflowing in eager testimony, as Andrew found Peter and Philip found Nathanael (John 1:41-45). The witnesses observe the self-denial in their haste: they thought nothing of the late hour, the spent day, the weariness of another long walk in the dark, nor of their own rest or refreshment — the joy of the news outweighing all; the love that counts no labour too great to share the good tidings. The expositors draw the lesson on the impulse of grace: the heart that has met the risen Christ longs to tell of him; the young convert hastens to proclaim what God has done for his soul (Psalm 66:16); the joy of salvation makes the believer eager to share it. The witnesses note that they "found the eleven gathered together, and them that were with them" — the disciples assembled in Jerusalem, the company of believers gathered (despite their fear and grief), to whom the two would bring their testimony and from whom they would hear the news of the appearance to Peter. The expositors observe the fellowship of the disciples: gathered together in the dark hour, sharing their hopes and fears, now to share the mounting evidence of the resurrection — the gathered church the place where the testimonies converge. The witnesses draw the application: the believer who has found Christ is to hasten, like these disciples, to share the joyful news, counting no labour too great; and to seek the fellowship of the gathered people, where the witness to the risen Lord is confirmed. The verse thus records the disciples' eager return — rising at once to carry the news — teaching the joy that cannot be contained, the eagerness to share the risen Christ, and the gathered fellowship that received their testimony.
34:
"Saying, The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon." The witnesses note the report of the appearance to Peter. Lightfoot establishes that these are the words of the eleven, greeting the two from Emmaus: "they found the eleven and the rest, saying... The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared unto Simon." The witnesses note this appearance to Peter (Simon), elsewhere noted by Paul (1 Corinthians 15:5, "he was seen of Cephas") — the risen Christ appearing to the fallen-and-restored Peter, a token of pardoning grace. The witnesses note the disciples confessing the certain truth of the resurrection.

The witnesses note the joyful confession: the Lord is risen indeed, and has appeared to Peter.

So the witnesses make the verse the report of the appearance to Peter. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty of the resurrection, confessed by the gathered disciples, and the grace shown in the appearance to the fallen Peter. The witnesses note the confession: "The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon" — and Lightfoot establishes that these are the words of the eleven, greeting the two returning from Emmaus; the assembled disciples now confessing the resurrection as certain, confirmed by the appearance to Peter. The expositors press the certainty in "risen indeed": the disciples, who had doubted and grieved, now affirm the resurrection as an established fact — not a rumour or a hope, but a truth confirmed by the Lord's appearing; the mounting evidence (the empty tomb, the angels, the appearance to Peter, and now the two from Emmaus) converging into assured confession. The witnesses observe the particular grace in the appearance to Peter: of the individual apostles, the risen Christ appeared first (so far as recorded) to Simon Peter — the very disciple who had denied him thrice; and Paul confirms it (1 Corinthians 15:5, "he was seen of Cephas"). The expositors draw the lesson on pardoning grace: that Christ should appear first to Peter, the fallen and restored, is a token of his tender forgiveness — seeking out the disciple who had denied him, assuring him of pardon and restoration; the risen Lord's first personal visit to the one who most needed comfort. The witnesses note the connection with Christ's prayer for Peter (Luke 22:32, "that thy faith fail not"): the appearance to Peter the fruit of that intercession, the fallen disciple sought, forgiven, and restored by the risen Christ. The expositors observe the encouragement this gives to all who have fallen: Christ does not cast off the penitent, but seeks them out, appears to them, restores them; the appearance to Peter the pledge that grace abounds to the fallen who return. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rejoices in the certainty of the resurrection, confessed by the gathered church, and takes comfort from the grace that appeared first to fallen Peter — that the risen Christ seeks and restores the penitent. The verse thus records the report of the appearance to Peter — the Lord is risen indeed — teaching the certainty of the resurrection and the pardoning grace shown in Christ's appearing to the fallen and restored disciple.
35:
"And they told what things were done in the way, and how he was known of them in breaking of bread." The witnesses note the two disciples adding their testimony. Gill notes that when the eleven had finished their report, the two from Emmaus "gave them a particular account, how, as they were travelling, Jesus joined himself to them... and opened the Scriptures." Ellicott observes Luke's use of the phrase "breaking of bread," a term that "had already acquired" a settled meaning by the time he wrote. The witnesses note the two recounting their encounter on the road and the recognition at the table — confirming the resurrection.

The witnesses note the two disciples adding their witness — the road, the opened Scriptures, the recognition in breaking bread.

So the witnesses make the verse the testimony of the two from Emmaus. The lesson the expositors draw is the convergence of the witnesses to the resurrection, and the recognition of Christ in the breaking of bread. The witnesses note that the two, having heard the eleven's report of the appearance to Peter, in turn "told what things were done in the way, and how he was known of them in breaking of bread" — recounting their own encounter: how the risen Christ had joined them on the road, opened the Scriptures to them, kindled their hearts, and was finally known to them in the breaking of bread. The expositors observe the convergence of testimony: the empty tomb, the angels' word, the appearance to Peter, and now the appearance on the Emmaus road — the witnesses multiplying and agreeing, building the assured confession that the Lord was risen indeed; each testimony confirming the rest.
