The witnesses note the particular point the two stress: "how he was known of them in breaking of bread" — the recognition came in that familiar act; and the expositors observe Ellicott's note that Luke's phrase "breaking of bread" had, by his writing, acquired a settled significance (the common term for the disciples' fellowship-meals, and the Lord's Supper, Acts 2:42) — though, as Lightfoot argued (v.30), the act at Emmaus was the ordinary blessing of a meal, not the sacrament. The expositors draw the lesson on the revealing of Christ: he was made known in the breaking of bread — in the act of fellowship and grace; and the believer may look for Christ to be known in the ordinary means and the fellowship of his people. The witnesses press the value of shared testimony: the disciples strengthened one another's faith by recounting what each had seen — the eleven the appearance to Peter, the two the encounter on the road; the church built up by the mutual witness to the risen Lord. The expositors observe the pattern for the gathered people: coming together, they shared the evidences of the resurrection, confirming and establishing one another in the faith — the fellowship the place where testimony is shared and faith strengthened. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to add his testimony to the witness of the church, recounting how Christ has been made known to him, and to find Christ revealed in the fellowship and the breaking of bread. The verse thus records the testimony of the two from Emmaus — the road and the breaking of bread — teaching the convergence of the witnesses to the resurrection, the recognition of Christ in the breaking of bread, and the mutual witness that builds up the gathered church.
36:
"And as they thus spake, Jesus himself stood in the midst of them, and saith unto them, Peace be unto you." The witnesses note Christ's sudden appearance and his greeting. Chrysostom notes that as the report of the resurrection spread and the disciples longed to see him, "He that was so much desired comes, and is revealed... not in a doubtful manner, but with the clearest evidence." Augustine harmonizes with John: Thomas, absent at this appearance (John 20:24), "departed from them before our Lord appeared," Luke's "the eleven" being used of the apostolic company generally. The witnesses note Christ standing suddenly in the midst, greeting them with peace.

The witnesses note Christ standing suddenly among the disciples, greeting them with "Peace be unto you."

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's appearance to the gathered disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the gracious self-manifestation of the risen Christ to his longing disciples, and the peace he brings. The witnesses note the suddenness: "as they thus spake" — even as they shared the testimonies of the resurrection — "Jesus himself stood in the midst of them"; the risen Christ appearing suddenly, the doors shut (John 20:19), standing among them with the clearest evidence of his presence. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note on the timing: as the disciples spoke of him and longed to see him, the much-desired Lord comes and reveals himself — answering their longing, manifesting himself to those who sought him; the risen Christ drawing near to his gathered, seeking people. The witnesses note the greeting: "Peace be unto you" — the customary Jewish salutation, but on the risen Christ's lips full of deeper meaning; the peace he had bequeathed them (John 14:27), the peace purchased by his cross, now spoken by the risen Lord — calming their fear, assuring them of his favour, and proclaiming the reconciliation his death had won. The expositors draw the lesson on the peace of the risen Christ: he comes to his troubled disciples not with rebuke for their unbelief and desertion, but with peace — the first word of the risen Lord a word of grace and reconciliation; the peace that flows from his finished work. The witnesses observe Augustine's harmony with John: Thomas was absent at this appearance (John 20:24), having departed before Christ came; Luke's "the eleven" (v.33) is used of the apostolic body in general, not requiring all to be present — a careful reconciliation of the accounts. The expositors note the assurance the appearance gave: the disciples, who had only heard the reports, now see the risen Christ themselves, standing among them; the testimony confirmed by sight, the longed-for Lord present in their midst. The witnesses draw the application: the risen Christ draws near to his gathered people, manifests himself to those who long for him, and brings peace to the troubled; the believer is to seek him in the fellowship and to receive the peace he speaks. The verse thus records Christ's appearance to the disciples — standing in the midst with peace — teaching the gracious self-manifestation of the risen Lord to his longing people, and the peace his finished work proclaims.
37:
"But they were terrified and affrighted, and supposed that they had seen a spirit." The witnesses note the disciples' fear and mistaken supposition. Gill notes they were terrified "at his sudden appearance among them, without being heard or seen before, and the doors shut"; they could not account for it, "though they had been just speaking of his resurrection." Lightfoot discusses the Jewish notion of "spirits" — souls without bodies, or apparitions of the deceased — supposing this was not Christ in the body but his ghost. The witnesses note the disciples, startled, taking the risen Christ for a disembodied spirit.

The witnesses note the disciples' terror — mistaking the risen Christ for a spirit or ghost.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' fear and mistaken supposition. The lesson the expositors draw is the weakness of their faith, slow to believe the bodily resurrection even with the evidence before them, and the occasion it gave for fuller proof. The witnesses note the reaction: "they were terrified and affrighted, and supposed that they had seen a spirit" — startled by Christ's sudden appearance (Gill notes, the doors shut, no sound of his coming), they were filled with fear, and took him not for the risen Lord in his body but for a spirit, a ghost, an apparition. The expositors observe the irony Gill marks: they had just been speaking of his resurrection, hearing and sharing the testimonies — yet when the risen Christ stood before them, they could not believe it was truly he in his body, but supposed a spirit; the weakness of faith persisting even amid the evidence. The witnesses note Lightfoot's explanation of the Jewish notion: they distinguished spirits (souls without bodies) and supposed apparitions of the dead under that name; the disciples imagined this was not Christ risen in his actual body, but his disembodied spirit or ghost appearing to them. The expositors draw the lesson on the slowness of faith: the disciples' fear and mistaken supposition show how hard it was for them to grasp the bodily resurrection — a token, the expositors note, of the genuineness of the resurrection faith, for the disciples did not credulously expect or imagine it, but were slow and doubtful, convinced only by overwhelming evidence. The witnesses press the apologetic value: the disciples' very unbelief proves they were no easy dupes, no inventors of a tale — they doubted, feared, supposed a ghost, and had to be convinced against their fears; the resurrection faith was forced on them by the evidence, not fancied by their hopes. The expositors observe the occasion this gave for fuller proof: Christ, finding them mistaking him for a spirit, would give them the clearest evidence of his bodily resurrection — his hands and feet, his flesh and bones, his eating before them (the verses to come). The witnesses draw the application: the believer is not to wonder at the slowness of faith, but to mark how Christ condescends to give full proof; and to rest on the bodily resurrection, established against the disciples' very doubts. The verse thus records the disciples' terror — supposing a spirit — teaching the weakness and slowness of their faith, the apologetic value of their doubt, and the occasion it gave for the fuller proof of the bodily resurrection.
38:
"And he said unto them, Why are ye troubled? and why do thoughts arise in your hearts?" The witnesses note Christ's gentle question and reproof. Barnes explains "thoughts" means "doubts or suspicions"; Christ "reproves them for doubting" whether he was the Christ, when the Scriptures had foretold his death, he had himself foretold it, and they now had Peter's testimony and the angels' word. Ellicott notes the question witnesses to "the identity of character of the risen Lord with all that had belonged to His humanity," he too having known what it was to be troubled. The witnesses note Christ asking why they are troubled and beset by doubts.

The witnesses note Christ's gentle reproof: why are they troubled and doubting, with all the evidence before them?

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's gentle question and reproof. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender reproof of the disciples' doubt, and the abiding identity of the risen Lord with the Jesus they had known. The witnesses note the question: "Why are ye troubled? and why do thoughts arise in your hearts?" — Christ, finding the disciples terrified and doubting, gently asks the cause of their fear and the rising of their doubts. The expositors observe that "thoughts" here means doubts or suspicions (Barnes), the questionings that rose in their hearts — whether this was truly Christ, whether he was risen, whether he was the Messiah they had hoped. The witnesses note the reproof implied: Christ reproves their doubting, for they had every ground to believe — the Scriptures had foretold his death and resurrection, he had himself repeatedly foretold it, and they now had the testimony of the empty tomb, the angels, Peter, and the two from Emmaus; after all this evidence, their doubt was without excuse. The expositors draw the lesson on the unreasonableness of unbelief: the disciples' doubt persisted against abundant evidence — Scripture, Christ's own word, and the converging testimonies; their trouble sprang not from want of proof but from the weakness and slowness of their faith. The witnesses press the gentleness of the reproof: Christ does not rebuke them harshly, but tenderly questions them — "why are ye troubled?" — as one who understands their weakness and would calm their fears; the reproof of a Shepherd, not a stern judge. The expositors observe Ellicott's note on the continuity of character: the question witnesses to the identity of the risen Lord with the Jesus of the ministry — the same tender, searching One who had so often questioned and comforted them; the risen Christ no stranger, but the same Jesus, in the same character of grace. The witnesses note the comfort in this: the risen Lord is the same who walked with them, knows their hearts, understands their trouble, and gently leads them from doubt to faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is gently reproved for doubting against the evidence, and is to mark the tender continuity of the risen Christ, the same Jesus who understands and calms our fears. The verse thus records Christ's gentle question — why are ye troubled? — teaching the tender reproof of doubt against abundant evidence, and the abiding identity of the risen Lord with the Jesus the disciples had known and loved.
39:
"Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see; for a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have." The witnesses note Christ's proof of his bodily resurrection. Gill notes the prints of the nails (John adds the side) "by which they might be convinced he was not a spirit, and be assured of the truth of his resurrection... in the same numerical body in which he suffered." Barnes stresses the evidence: "he appealed to their senses, and performed acts which a disembodied spirit could not do." The witnesses note Christ inviting them to see and handle his hands and feet — proof that he was risen in a true body of flesh and bones.

The witnesses note Christ's proof: his hands, feet, flesh and bones — the reality of the bodily resurrection.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's proof of his bodily resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality and identity of the risen body — flesh and bones, the same body that suffered — established against the disciples' supposition of a spirit. The witnesses note the invitation: "Behold my hands and my feet, that it is I myself: handle me, and see" — Christ offers the clearest sensory proof, showing the prints of the nails in his hands and feet (and his side, John 20:20, 27), and bidding them touch him; the appeal to both sight and touch. The expositors press the argument: "a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have" — Christ confutes their supposition that he was a ghost, for a disembodied spirit has no flesh and bones, no body to be handled; but he has — they may see and feel it; therefore he is no spirit, but risen in a true, tangible body. The witnesses observe the identity established by the wounds: the prints of the nails and spear prove it is the same body that was crucified — "it is I myself"; not a new body, nor a phantom, but the very body that suffered, now risen and glorified; the scars the marks of identity and the tokens of his love (Gill notes), enduring even in the risen body. The expositors draw the great doctrine of the bodily resurrection: Christ rose not as a spirit, nor in appearance only, but in a real body of flesh and bones — the same body, transformed and glorified, yet truly material and identifiable; the foundation of the Christian hope of the resurrection of the body. The witnesses note the apologetic force Barnes marks: Christ appealed to their senses, performed acts a disembodied spirit could not — being seen, handled, and (the verses to come) eating; no better evidence could be given, the resurrection established beyond cavil against docetism and every denial of the bodily rising. The expositors observe the comfort in the enduring wounds: the risen Christ bears still the marks of his Passion, the tokens of his love for his people, the evidence of the price he paid; the wounds that prove his identity also proclaim his redeeming love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the bodily resurrection of Christ, proved by his hands and feet and flesh and bones; and hopes for the resurrection of his own body, after the pattern of his risen Lord. The verse thus records Christ's proof of his bodily resurrection — handle me, a spirit hath not flesh and bones — teaching the reality and identity of the risen body, the foundation of the resurrection hope, and the enduring wounds that prove his love.
40:
"And when he had thus spoken, he shewed them his hands and his feet." The witnesses note Christ's showing of his wounds. Gill notes that, having invited them, Christ "put them into a method of satisfying themselves by their senses." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the verse is "a Western non-interpolation according to Westcott and Hort," but "genuine in John 20:20." The witnesses note Christ visibly showing the disciples the prints in his hands and feet — confirming by sight the proof he had offered.

The witnesses note Christ showing his wounded hands and feet — the visible proof of his risen body.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's showing of his wounds. The lesson the expositors draw is the condescension of Christ in furnishing visible, tangible proof of his bodily resurrection. The witnesses note that, having invited them to behold and handle him (v.39), Christ "shewed them his hands and his feet" — visibly displaying the prints of the nails, putting them (Gill notes) in the way of satisfying themselves by their own senses; the proof not merely offered in word but shown to their eyes. The expositors observe the condescension of Christ: he does not require the disciples to believe without evidence, but graciously furnishes the fullest proof — showing his wounded hands and feet, that they might see with their own eyes the marks of his crucifixion in his risen body. The witnesses note the textual observation Robertson makes: the verse is reckoned by some editors a "Western non-interpolation" (a passage absent from the Western text and possibly added), yet the same showing of the hands and side is firmly attested in John 20:20; so the substance — Christ showing his wounds — stands certain, whatever the textual standing of this particular verse in Luke. The expositors draw the lesson on the evidence of the wounds: the displayed hands and feet proved both the reality of his body (a spirit has none to show) and its identity (the same body that was nailed to the cross); the visible wounds the unanswerable confirmation of the resurrection. The witnesses press the patience and grace of Christ: dealing tenderly with the disciples' weak faith, he gives them every help to believe — word, sight, touch, and (soon) the eating of food; meeting their doubt not with rebuke but with proof. The expositors observe the abiding significance of the shown wounds: the risen and glorified Christ bears still the marks of his Passion, displayed to his disciples then and a pledge of his love for ever; the wounds shown the tokens of the redemption accomplished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the proof Christ condescended to give — the shown hands and feet — and adores the grace that meets weak faith with full evidence. The verse thus records Christ's showing of his wounds — his hands and feet — teaching the condescension that furnished visible proof of the risen body, and the enduring wounds that confirm both its reality and its identity.
41:
"And while they yet believed not for joy, and wondered, he said unto them, Have ye here any meat?" The witnesses note the disciples' joyful disbelief and Christ's request for food. Cyril notes Christ, having shown his hands and feet, "to confirm them still more, asked for something to eat." The witnesses note the striking phrase "believed not for joy" — the news too good to be at once believed, joy itself making them slow to credit it (cf. the like note on the women's sleep "for sorrow," Luke 22:45). Gregory of Nyssa sees in the food a sign of the resurrection's sweetness. The witnesses note Christ asking for food, to give yet further proof.

The witnesses note the disciples' joyful disbelief, and Christ's asking for food to confirm them further.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' joyful disbelief and Christ's request for food. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender psychology of joy too great to believe, and Christ's gracious furnishing of yet further proof. The witnesses note the striking phrase: "they yet believed not for joy, and wondered" — the disciples, overwhelmed with the joy of seeing the risen Christ, could not yet fully believe it; the news was too good, too wonderful, and joy itself made them slow to credit what they saw — a true and tender touch of human nature, that great joy, like great sorrow, can stagger belief. The expositors observe the like note Luke gives elsewhere — the women slept "for sorrow" (Luke 22:45) — the evangelist's eye for the workings of the heart; here joy, there sorrow, each affecting the mind; the disciples' disbelief not the hardness of unbelief but the bewilderment of overwhelming gladness. The witnesses note Christ's response to this lingering wonder: "Have ye here any meat?" — to confirm them still more (Cyril), he asks for food, that by eating before them he might give yet further, undeniable proof of his bodily resurrection. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's condescension to weak faith: he does not chide their slowness, but graciously adds proof to proof — having been seen and handled, he will now eat before them, that no doubt remain; the patience of Christ meeting their joyful disbelief with fuller evidence. The witnesses observe Gregory of Nyssa's reflection on the food (the next verse): the resurrection meal, sweetened with the honeycomb, a sign of the joy and sweetness of the risen life, after the bitter herbs of the Passover of suffering. The expositors note the reality the request would prove: a spirit needs no food, cannot eat; but Christ, asking for and eating food, would prove his true bodily nature beyond all question. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is comforted that Christ deals tenderly with joy and weakness alike, adding proof to proof; and learns that the joy of the resurrection may, at first, overwhelm belief, yet is confirmed by the fullest evidence. The verse thus records the disciples' joyful disbelief and Christ's request for food — teaching the tender psychology of joy too great to believe, and the gracious condescension of Christ in furnishing yet further proof of his risen body.
42:
"And they gave him a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb." The witnesses note the food given to the risen Christ. Ellicott notes the fact "points to the common food of the disciples" — fish (as in the feedings and John 21:9) "the staple article of diet," and honey, with which "Canaan was described as a land flowing with milk and honey." Gregory of Nyssa draws the symbolic contrast: "by the command of the law the Passover was eaten with bitter herbs, because the bitterness of bondage still remained, but after the resurrection the food is sweetened with a honeycomb." The witnesses note the broiled fish and honeycomb, the disciples' own fare, given to Christ.

The witnesses note the simple food — broiled fish and honeycomb — given to the risen Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the food given to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the homely reality of the scene and the sweetness of the risen life it figured. The witnesses note the food: "a piece of a broiled fish, and of an honeycomb" — the simple, common fare of the disciples, the fishermen's ordinary diet (Ellicott notes fish the staple, as in the feedings and the lakeside meal of John 21:9, and honey a common food in the land "flowing with milk and honey"); what was ready to hand, given to the risen Christ. The expositors observe the homely reality of the scene: the risen Lord, having proved his body by sight and touch, now asks for and receives ordinary food — broiled fish and honeycomb; the very plainness of the fare a token of the truth of the account, no invented or symbolic banquet, but the real meal of real disciples. The witnesses note Gregory of Nyssa's symbolic reflection: the contrast between the Passover and the resurrection meal — the Passover lamb eaten with bitter herbs, because the bitterness of bondage and suffering still remained; but after the resurrection, the food is sweetened with a honeycomb, signifying the joy and sweetness of the risen life, the bitterness past, the gladness come. The expositors draw the lesson, while holding the literal first: the honeycomb of the resurrection meal may fitly figure the sweetness that the resurrection brings — the joy after sorrow, the sweetness after the bitter, the gladness of the risen life; though the food was real and ordinary, it may yet carry this happy significance. The witnesses observe the condescension of Christ in the homely scene: the risen Lord, who needed no food, accepts the disciples' simple fare, sharing their common meal — the glorified Christ stooping to the ordinary fellowship of the table, that he might prove his body and comfort his people. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the reality of the risen body, proved by the ordinary food; and may find in the honeycomb a figure of the sweetness of the risen life and the joy of fellowship with the risen Lord. The verse thus records the food given to Christ — broiled fish and honeycomb — teaching the homely reality of the resurrection scene, and (with Gregory) the sweetness of the risen life figured in the honeycomb after the bitter herbs of the Passover.
43:
"And he took it, and did eat before them." The witnesses note Christ eating as the final proof of his bodily resurrection. Bede explains he ate "not from necessity, but by His power" — "the thirsty earth absorbs water from want, the burning sun from power" — Christ eating not because his glorified body needed food, but to prove its reality. Calvin marks his condescension: "how kindly Christ bears with the weakness of his followers... though he has obtained a new and heavenly life, and has no more need of meat than the angels, still he voluntarily condescends to join in the common usages of mortals." The witnesses note Christ eating before them — the conclusive proof against the supposition of a spirit.

The witnesses note Christ eating before them — the conclusive proof of his risen body, done by power not need.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's eating as the conclusive proof. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality of the risen body, proved by eating, done not from necessity but by power, in gracious condescension to weak faith. The witnesses note the act: "he took it, and did eat before them" — Christ, in the disciples' sight, ate the fish and honeycomb; the conclusive, undeniable proof that he was no spirit but truly risen in a real body, for a spirit cannot eat (Acts 10:41, "who did eat and drink with him after he rose"). The expositors press the nature of this eating, which Bede explains: Christ ate "not from necessity, but by His power" — his glorified body needed no food, as the angels need none and as the heavenly life requires none; yet he truly ate, exercising the power to take food, to prove the reality of his body. Bede's figure: the thirsty earth absorbs water from want, the burning sun from power — the one from need, the other freely; so Christ ate, not from hunger or necessity, but by his power, to confirm his disciples. The witnesses observe Calvin's note on the condescension: how kindly Christ bears with the weakness of his followers — though risen to a new and heavenly life, needing food no more than the angels, he yet voluntarily joins in the common usages of mortals, eating before them to support their failing faith; the glorified Lord stooping to the ordinary act to help their weakness. The expositors draw the lesson on the reality of the resurrection: the eating is the final, irrefutable proof — the disciples saw him eat, an act no ghost or phantom could perform; the bodily resurrection established beyond all doubt, against every denial. The witnesses note the comfort and the grace: Christ, who needed nothing, condescended to eat for their sakes — meeting their weak faith with the fullest proof, patient with their slowness, gracious in his condescension; the risen Lord caring for his disciples' assurance. The expositors observe the foundation this lays: the bodily resurrection of Christ, so fully proved, is the ground of the gospel and the pledge of the believer's own resurrection. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the risen Christ who ate before his disciples — the reality of the resurrection proved beyond cavil; and adores the condescension that met weak faith with such grace. The verse thus records Christ's eating before them — the conclusive proof — teaching the reality of the risen body, the eating done by power not necessity, and the gracious condescension of Christ to the weakness of his people.
44:
"And he said unto them, These are the words which I spake unto you, while I was yet with you, that all things must be fulfilled, which were written in the law of Moses, and in the prophets, and in the psalms, concerning me." The witnesses note Christ's appeal to the whole Old Testament. Lightfoot details "the law of Moses, the prophets, and the psalms" as "a known division of the Old Testament into the Law, the Prophets, and the Holy Writings" — the threefold Hebrew canon, the Psalms naming the third division (the Writings). The witnesses note Christ reminding them that all he had foretold, and all the Scriptures testified of him, must be fulfilled — his sufferings and glory written throughout the whole Old Testament.

The witnesses note Christ's appeal to the whole Old Testament — Law, Prophets, and Psalms — as testifying of him.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's appeal to the whole Old Testament. The lesson the expositors draw is that the entire Hebrew Scripture testifies of Christ, and that all written of him must be fulfilled. The witnesses note Christ's reminder: "these are the words which I spake unto you, while I was yet with you" — recalling that he had foretold, during his ministry, that all things written of him must be fulfilled; the resurrection he had repeatedly predicted, now come to pass, was the confirmation of all his word. The expositors observe the comprehensive appeal to Scripture: "the law of Moses, and the prophets, and the psalms" — Lightfoot details this as the known threefold division of the Hebrew Bible: the Law (the five books of Moses), the Prophets (the historical and prophetic books), and the Writings (named here by their chief book, the Psalms); so Christ appeals to the whole Old Testament, every part testifying of him. The witnesses press the truth this establishes: the entire Old Testament — its law, its prophecy, its psalms — concerns Christ; he is the theme and substance of all the Scriptures, foreshadowed in the law, foretold by the prophets, sung in the psalms; "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy" (Revelation 19:10). The expositors draw the lesson on the unity of Scripture: the Old and New Testaments are one, the former testifying beforehand of Christ, the latter recording his fulfilment; and the right reading of the Old Testament finds Christ throughout — in the types, the prophecies, the promises, the psalms. The witnesses note the necessity Christ presses: "all things must be fulfilled" — what the Scriptures foretold of him could not fail, but must come to pass; his sufferings, death, and resurrection the necessary fulfilment of the written word; the "must" of divine purpose and prophetic certainty. The expositors observe the use Christ makes of this: to confirm the disciples' faith by showing that his death and resurrection were no surprise nor defeat, but the foretold and necessary fulfilment of the Scriptures they had long held; the cross and the empty tomb the accomplishment of Moses, the prophets, and the psalms. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to read the whole Old Testament as testifying of Christ, to find him in all the Scriptures, and to rest on the certainty that all written of him is fulfilled. The verse thus records Christ's appeal to the whole Old Testament — Law, Prophets, and Psalms — teaching that all the Scriptures testify of Christ, the unity of the written word, and the necessary fulfilment of all that was written concerning him.
45:
"Then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures." The witnesses note the opening of the mind to understand the word. Bede notes the progression: "after presenting Himself to be seen and handled, and bringing to their minds the Scriptures, He next opened their understanding that they should understand what was read." Theophylact stresses its necessity: "otherwise, how would their agitated and perplexed minds have learnt the mystery of Christ?" Calvin argues against those who think "outward doctrine superfluous" if we have natural power to understand — for the Lord here both speaks the word and opens the mind. The witnesses note Christ opening the disciples' understanding to grasp the Scriptures.

The witnesses note the opening of the understanding — Christ giving the inward light to grasp the Scriptures.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the understanding. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saving understanding of Scripture is the gift of Christ, who opens the mind to grasp what the word reveals. The witnesses note the act: "then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures" — Christ, having appealed to the Scriptures (v.44), now opens the disciples' minds to understand them; the inward illumination by which the written word is savingly grasped. The expositors observe Bede's marking of the progression: Christ first presented himself to be seen and handled (the proof of the resurrection), then brought the Scriptures to their minds (the external word), and now opened their understanding (the inward light) — the full method of bringing them to faith: the fact, the word, and the illumination. The witnesses press Theophylact's note on the necessity: without this opening, their agitated and perplexed minds could not have grasped the mystery of Christ; the natural mind, even with the Scriptures before it, cannot understand the things of God without the inward enlightening — "the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God... neither can he know them" (1 Corinthians 2:14). The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of divine illumination: the right understanding of Scripture is not the mere work of natural intelligence, but the gift of Christ, who opens the understanding; the same Lord who gives the word must open the mind to receive it. The witnesses note Calvin's argument against those who would make the outward word superfluous: that Christ both speaks the Scriptures and opens the understanding shows that both are needed — the external word and the internal light; neither the word without the Spirit's opening, nor the opening without the word, but both together; God uses the outward doctrine and gives the inward understanding. The expositors observe the balance this teaches: the believer is to attend diligently to the Scriptures (the outward word) and to pray for the opening of his understanding (the inward light); for the word read without illumination profits not, and the Spirit works through the word, not apart from it. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to seek from Christ the opening of his understanding, that he may grasp the Scriptures; and to value both the written word and the illuminating Spirit, joined in the one work of bringing the soul to know Christ. The verse thus records the opening of the understanding — that they might understand the Scriptures — teaching that the saving grasp of the word is Christ's gift, the necessity of divine illumination, and the joining of the outward word with the inward light.
46:
"And said unto them, Thus it is written, and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day:" The witnesses note the necessity of Christ's suffering and resurrection. Barnes explains "it behoved" — "it became proper or necessary that the Messiah should thus suffer; it was predicted of him, and all things have happened as foretold" (Isaiah 53:3-5; Acts 4:12). Robertson notes Jesus now finds in the Old Testament "his suffering, his resurrection, and the preaching of repentance and forgiveness to all nations." The witnesses note Christ declaring that his suffering and rising on the third day were the necessary fulfilment of Scripture.

The witnesses note the necessity: Scripture foretold that Christ must suffer and rise the third day.

So the witnesses make the verse the necessity of Christ's suffering and resurrection. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cross and the empty tomb were the foretold and necessary fulfilment of Scripture, not defeat but the appointed way of redemption. The witnesses note Christ's summary of what is written: "thus it is written, and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day" — the Scriptures foretold both his suffering and his resurrection, and both must come to pass; the "behoved" marking the divine necessity. The expositors observe the meaning of "it behoved" that Barnes draws: it became proper, fitting, necessary that the Messiah should suffer and rise — necessary because predicted (Isaiah 53), and necessary because the redemption of sinners required it; the cross not an accident or a defeat, but the appointed and indispensable way of salvation. The witnesses press the two parts: Christ "must suffer" — his death the foretold atonement, the suffering Servant bearing the sin of many; and "must rise from the dead the third day" — his resurrection the foretold vindication and victory, completing the work and proving its acceptance. The expositors draw the lesson on the cross as foretold and necessary: the disciples, who had been confounded by Christ's death as the ruin of their hopes, are now taught that it was the very fulfilment of Scripture — the Messiah must suffer; the cross the appointed path to glory, not its denial. The witnesses note Robertson's observation that Christ finds in the Old Testament his suffering, his resurrection, and the gospel commission (the next verse) — the whole gospel written beforehand, fulfilled in him. The expositors observe the comfort this gave the disciples: their Lord's death and resurrection were no surprise to God, no failure of the plan, but its accomplishment; the Scriptures had foretold it, and it must be so — the cross and the rising the foreordained way of redemption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the necessity of Christ's suffering and resurrection — foretold, fitting, indispensable; the cross the appointed atonement, the rising the appointed victory, both the fulfilment of the written word. The verse thus records the necessity of Christ's suffering and resurrection — thus it behoved — teaching that the cross and the empty tomb were the foretold and necessary fulfilment of Scripture, the appointed way of redemption.
47:
"And that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem." The witnesses note the gospel commission to all nations. Gill expounds the sum of the gospel: repentance "a doctrine of the Gospel... a gift of God's grace," and remission of sins, which "springs from the free grace of God" but "is procured by the blood of Christ"; the two joined, "not because repentance is the cause of pardon" — for "repentance makes no satisfaction for sin" — but the blood of Christ alone the meritorious cause. The witnesses note the gospel of repentance and forgiveness to be preached in Christ's name to all nations, beginning at Jerusalem.

The witnesses note the gospel commission: repentance and remission preached in Christ's name to all nations.

So the witnesses make the verse the gospel commission. The lesson the expositors draw is the sum of the gospel — repentance and remission of sins in Christ's name — and its universal reach to all nations. The witnesses note the content of the commission: "repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name" — the two great themes of the gospel ministry; repentance, the turning from sin, and remission, the forgiveness of sin, both to be proclaimed. The expositors press Gill's careful theology of their relation: repentance and remission are joined, yet not as cause and effect — repentance is not the cause or ground of pardon, for repentance makes no satisfaction for sin, the tears of the penitent cannot wash it away; rather, remission springs from the free grace of God and is procured solely by the blood of Christ, the only meritorious cause; repentance and forgiveness joined as the gifts of grace that go together, but the blood of Christ alone the ground of pardon. The witnesses note that both are preached "in his name" — in the name of Christ, by his authority, on the ground of his merit; repentance and remission are gifts in his hand to bestow, proclaimed in his name and received through faith in him. The expositors draw the lesson on the universal reach: "among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem" — the gospel is for all peoples, not the Jews only; the commission embraces the whole world, the middle wall broken down, the nations called to repentance and pardon. The witnesses observe the grace in "beginning at Jerusalem": the gospel was to start at the very city that crucified Christ — offering pardon first to his murderers; the riches of grace, that the first offer of forgiveness should go to those most guilty of his blood (fulfilled at Pentecost, Acts 2). The expositors note the order of the commission: from Jerusalem outward, to Judea, Samaria, and the uttermost parts (Acts 1:8); the gospel spreading from the center to all nations, as the apostles would carry it. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rejoices in the gospel of repentance and remission, freely offered in Christ's name to all nations; rests pardon on the blood of Christ alone, not on his repentance; and marvels at the grace that began at Jerusalem. The verse thus records the gospel commission — repentance and remission in Christ's name to all nations — teaching the sum of the gospel, the ground of pardon in Christ's blood, and the universal reach of grace beginning even at Jerusalem.
48:
"And ye are witnesses of these things." The witnesses note the apostles' commission as witnesses. Gill details what they witnessed: the truth of Christ's humanity, his miracles, his sufferings and death, and above all "his resurrection from the dead, in the same body, to whom he showed himself alive by many infallible proofs" — and their business to "testify the doctrine of repentance and remission of sins" to all nations. Barnes notes they were witnesses "of my life, my sufferings" and resurrection. The witnesses note the apostles appointed to testify what they had seen and heard of Christ.

The witnesses note the apostles' commission: to be witnesses of Christ's life, death, and resurrection.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' commission as witnesses. The lesson the expositors draw is the office of the apostles as the appointed witnesses of Christ, qualified by what they had seen and heard. The witnesses note the charge: "ye are witnesses of these things" — the apostles appointed to testify of Christ; their office to bear witness to what they had personally seen and known. The expositors detail, with Gill, the scope of their testimony: they were witnesses of the truth of Christ's humanity (having seen, heard, and handled him), of his miracles, of his sufferings and death, and above all of his resurrection — to which he showed himself alive by many infallible proofs (Acts 1:3), seen, handled, eating before them; and their business was also to testify the doctrine of repentance and remission of sins to all nations. The witnesses press the qualification of the apostles as witnesses: they testified not hearsay nor speculation, but what they had themselves seen and known — eyewitnesses of the risen Christ, the ground of the gospel testimony being the personal witness of those who saw him alive; "that which we have seen and heard declare we unto you" (1 John 1:3). The expositors draw the lesson on the foundation of the gospel: it rests on the testimony of credible eyewitnesses — the apostles who saw the risen Lord and bore witness at the cost of their lives; the faith built on their attested testimony, not on myth or invention. The witnesses observe the dignity and burden of the office: to be Christ's witnesses is a high calling and a solemn charge — to testify of him before the world, to proclaim his death and resurrection, to bear witness even unto death (the word "witness," martys, becoming "martyr"); the apostles faithful to this charge, sealing their testimony with their blood. The expositors note the connection with the commission (v.47): the apostles are to preach repentance and remission to all nations, as witnesses of the Christ in whose name they are preached; their witness the means by which the gospel goes forth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rests on the apostolic testimony to the risen Christ, and is himself called, in his measure, to witness to what he has known of the Lord; every Christian a witness of Christ. The verse thus records the apostles' commission as witnesses — ye are witnesses — teaching the office of the apostles as eyewitnesses of Christ, the foundation of the gospel on their attested testimony, and the calling to bear witness to the risen Lord.
49:
"And, behold, I send the promise of my Father upon you: but tarry ye in the city of Jerusalem, until ye be endued with power from on high." The witnesses note the promise of the Spirit and the charge to wait. Gill explains "the promise of my Father" as "the promise of the Spirit" — called the Father's promise because promised by him and poured out in the Messiah's times; to be sent "in ten days time," sitting on them in fire at Pentecost; and the apostles to "tarry" until "endued with power from on high" — qualified and assisted for their witness. The witnesses note Christ promising the Spirit and charging the apostles to wait at Jerusalem until empowered.

The witnesses note the promise of the Spirit — the apostles to wait at Jerusalem until clothed with power from on high.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of the Spirit and the charge to wait. The lesson the expositors draw is the necessity of the Spirit's power for the work of witness, and the duty of waiting for the promised endowment. The witnesses note the promise: "I send the promise of my Father upon you" — the gift of the Holy Spirit, called "the promise of the Father" (Gill notes) because promised by the Father and poured out in the days of the Messiah; the Spirit to come upon the apostles, as he did at Pentecost ten days later, sitting on them in tongues of fire (Acts 2:3). The expositors press the necessity of this gift: the apostles, though witnesses of the risen Christ, were not yet fit for their great work; they needed to be "endued with power from on high" — clothed, invested with the Spirit's power, qualified and assisted for their witness; without the Spirit, their testimony would lack power, but filled with him, they would turn the world upside down. The witnesses note the charge to wait: "tarry ye in the city of Jerusalem, until ye be endued with power" — the apostles were not to begin their work at once, but to wait, in prayer and expectation, until the Spirit came; the duty of waiting for the promised power before the work. The expositors draw the lesson on dependence for the Spirit's power: all true service of Christ must be done in the power of the Spirit, not in the strength of nature; the apostles, for all their gifts and their witness, must wait to be clothed with power from on high — a lesson to all who would serve Christ, to wait on God for his enabling. The witnesses observe the order: first the promise, then the waiting, then the power, then the work — the Spirit's enduement the indispensable preparation for the gospel mission; the apostles to be filled before they were sent forth in power. The expositors note the encouragement: the work to which they were called was beyond their natural strength, but the promised Spirit would supply all — the power from on high making weak men mighty witnesses; the gospel's advance the work of the Spirit through the waiting, empowered apostles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to depend on the Spirit's power for all service, to wait on God for his enabling, and to undertake no work for Christ in mere natural strength; the power from on high the necessity for fruitful witness. The verse thus records the promise of the Spirit and the charge to wait — tarry until endued with power — teaching the necessity of the Spirit's power for the work, the duty of waiting for the promised endowment, and the dependence on God's enabling for all true service.
50:
"And he led them out as far as to Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them." The witnesses note the leading out to Bethany and the blessing. Bede notes Luke joins the resurrection day to the ascension day (omitting the forty days), and draws the symbolism: Bethany means "the house of obedience" — "He who descended because of the disobedience of the wicked, ascended because of the obedience of the converted"; and being on the side of Olivet, the house of the obedient Church is founded on Christ. Theophylact sees in the blessing the pouring of "a power of preservation, until the coming of the Spirit." The witnesses note Christ leading the disciples to Bethany and lifting up his hands to bless them as he departed.

The witnesses note Christ leading the disciples to Bethany and blessing them with uplifted hands.

So the witnesses make the verse the leading out and the blessing before the ascension. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender parting of Christ, who left his disciples with a blessing, and the fitness of the place. The witnesses note that Christ "led them out as far as to Bethany" — to the familiar village near the Mount of Olives, the place of his friends and his frequent resort; and there, on the slope of Olivet, prepared to ascend. The expositors observe Bede's note that Luke joins the resurrection day to the ascension, passing over the forty days between (recounted in Acts 1:3) — compressing the account, the Gospel's brief close; and his symbolic reading: Bethany, "the house of obedience," fittingly the place of the ascension — for he who came down because of the disobedience of the wicked ascended because of the obedience of the converted; the obedient Church founded on Christ, on the side of the highest mountain. The witnesses note the blessing: "he lifted up his hands, and blessed them" — Christ, departing, lifts his hands (as the priest of old, Leviticus 9:22) and blesses his disciples; his last act on earth a blessing, parting from them not with rebuke nor mere farewell, but with the priestly benediction of his grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the manner of Christ's departure: he left his people blessing them — and (the next verse) was taken up in the very act of blessing; so that the blessing was, as it were, never finished, but continues — the ascended Christ ever blessing his people from heaven. The witnesses observe Theophylact's reflection that the blessing poured into them a power of preservation until the Spirit should come, and taught them to commend others to God by blessing. The expositors note the tenderness of the parting: the disciples, soon to be left, receive their Lord's blessing — the assurance of his continued grace and favour; the ascension no abandonment, but a parting in blessing, the Lord going to prepare a place and to bless from on high. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is comforted that Christ parted from his people in blessing, and blesses them still from heaven; and is to mark the fitness of the house of obedience as the place of his ascension. The verse thus records the leading out and the blessing — Christ lifting his hands at Bethany — teaching the tender parting of the Lord who left his disciples blessing them, and the priestly benediction that continues from the ascended Christ.
51:
"And it came to pass, while he blessed them, he was parted from them, and carried up into heaven." The witnesses note the ascension of Christ. Clarke notes he was "carried up into that heaven from which he had descended" (John 3:13), forty days after the resurrection (Acts 1:3), having given convincing proofs (seen by over 500, 1 Corinthians 15:6); in the ascension the disciples had "the fullest proof of the immortality of the soul, the resurrection of the body, and his continual intercession." Ellicott notes the words "and carried up into heaven" are "wanting in some of the best MSS, and omitted accordingly by some recent editors." The witnesses note Christ taken up to heaven in the act of blessing.

The witnesses note the ascension — Christ carried up to heaven in the very act of blessing his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the ascension of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the glorious return of Christ to heaven, in the act of blessing, and the proofs and comforts his ascension affords. The witnesses note the manner: "while he blessed them, he was parted from them, and carried up into heaven" — Christ ascended in the very act of blessing his disciples; lifted up before their eyes (Acts 1:9), returning to the heaven from which he had come (John 3:13). The expositors observe the significance Clarke draws: Christ was carried up into that heaven from which he had descended; the ascension the completion of his earthly work — having lived to show the way, died to pay the price, risen to conquer death, he now ascends to take his place at the Father's right hand. The witnesses note the proofs the ascension afforded: it came forty days after the resurrection (Acts 1:3), during which Christ had given many convincing proofs of his risen life, seen by above five hundred at once (1 Corinthians 15:6); and in the visible ascension the disciples had (Clarke notes) the fullest proof of the immortality of the soul, the resurrection of the body, and Christ's continual intercession at the Father's right hand. The expositors draw the lesson on the comforts of the ascension: Christ ascended as the forerunner of his people, to prepare a place for them (John 14:2); as their High Priest, to intercede for them (Hebrews 7:25); and as their King, to reign until all enemies are subdued; the ascension the ground of the believer's hope and the pledge of his own ascending. The witnesses note Ellicott's textual observation, honestly recorded: the words "and carried up into heaven" are absent from some of the best manuscripts and omitted by some editors — though the ascension itself is firmly attested in Acts 1:9-11 and throughout the New Testament; the fact secure, whatever the standing of this clause in Luke's Gospel. The expositors observe the fitness of the ascension in the act of blessing: Christ's last posture toward his people was blessing, and so he remains — the ascended Lord ever blessing, his hands still, as it were, uplifted over his Church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer rejoices in the ascended Christ — his forerunner, High Priest, and King — and rests on the proofs and comforts the ascension affords. The verse thus records the ascension of Christ — carried up while blessing — teaching the glorious return of the Lord to heaven, the proofs of his risen life, and the comforts of his ascension as forerunner, intercessor, and King.
52:
"And they worshipped him, and returned to Jerusalem with great joy:" The witnesses note the disciples' worship and joy. Gill stresses the worship was religious, not merely civil: they worshipped Christ "with religious worship as God; for by his resurrection... and by his ascension to heaven, the disciples were more confirmed in his proper deity, and divine sonship." Barnes marks its significance: "they offered this worship to an absent Saviour... after he had left them; it was therefore an act of religion, and was the first religious" act after the ascension. The witnesses note the disciples worshipping the ascended Christ and returning with great joy.

The witnesses note the disciples worshipping the ascended Christ as God, and returning with great joy.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' worship and joy. The lesson the expositors draw is the religious worship of the ascended Christ, confirming his deity, and the great joy that the resurrection and ascension brought. The witnesses note the first act after the ascension: "they worshipped him" — the disciples, beholding Christ taken up, fell to worship; and Gill stresses this was religious worship, the worship due to God, not the mere civil homage paid to a great man — for by the resurrection and ascension the disciples were confirmed in Christ's proper deity and divine sonship, and now worshipped him as God. The expositors press the significance Barnes marks: the worship was offered to an absent Saviour, after he had left them and ascended out of sight — and was therefore an act of religion, the first such worship of the ascended Christ; the disciples adoring the Lord now exalted to heaven, owning his Godhead by their worship. The witnesses draw the lesson on the deity of Christ: the disciples' worship is a witness to his divinity — for worship is due to God alone, and the disciples, who had been Jews zealous against idolatry, now worship Jesus as God; the resurrection and ascension having declared him the Son of God with power (Romans 1:4). The expositors observe the great joy: "returned to Jerusalem with great joy" — not sorrow at his departure, but joy; for they understood now that his ascension was his glory and their gain — he gone to the Father, to prepare their place and to send the Spirit; the joy of the resurrection and ascension dispelling the grief of the cross. The witnesses note the change from the disciples' former sorrow: where they had mourned at his death and feared in the dark hours, now they rejoice — the days of their mourning ended (Isaiah 60:20), the joy of the risen and ascended Lord filling their hearts. The expositors draw the lesson on the joy of the ascension: the departure of Christ, rightly understood, is cause for joy, not grief — for he is exalted, reigning, interceding, and will send the Spirit and come again; the believer's joy founded on the ascended Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer worships the ascended Christ as God, and rejoices in his exaltation; the worship and joy of the disciples the pattern for the Church's adoration of her ascended Lord. The verse thus records the disciples' worship and joy — worshipping the ascended Christ, returning with great joy — teaching the religious worship that confirms his deity, and the joy that the resurrection and ascension brought.
53:
"And were continually in the temple, praising and blessing God. Amen." The witnesses note the disciples' continual worship — the joyful close of the Gospel. Gill notes they were in the temple "at the time of worship, at the hours of prayer... and perhaps always privately in an upper room," continuing "with one accord in prayer and supplication." Barnes marks the close: continually in the temple until Pentecost (Acts 2), "praising and blessing God" chiefly for the proof that the Messiah had come, redeemed them, and ascended — "thus the days of their mourning were ended." The witnesses note the disciples continually praising God in the temple, awaiting the Spirit.

The witnesses note the joyful close: the disciples continually in the temple, praising and blessing God.

So the witnesses make the verse the joyful close of the Gospel. The lesson the expositors draw is the continual worship and joy of the disciples, awaiting the promised Spirit, and the fitting end of Luke's Gospel in praise. The witnesses note the disciples' practice: "continually in the temple, praising and blessing God" — they were constantly in the temple at the hours of worship and prayer (Gill notes), and gathered also in the upper room, continuing with one accord in prayer and supplication (Acts 1:14); their life now one of worship, joy, and expectation. The expositors observe the obedience to Christ's charge: he had bidden them tarry at Jerusalem until endued with power (v.49); and they obey, waiting in worship and prayer, until the Spirit should come at Pentecost (Acts 2) — the interval filled not with idleness but with continual praise and supplication. The witnesses press the theme of joy that crowns the Gospel: the disciples are "praising and blessing God" — Barnes notes, chiefly for the proof that the Messiah had come, had redeemed them, and had ascended to heaven; "thus the days of their mourning were ended" (Isaiah 60:20); the grief of the cross swallowed up in the joy of the resurrection and ascension. The expositors draw the lesson on the fitting close: Luke's Gospel, which opened in the temple (with Zacharias, Luke 1) and with songs of praise (Mary, Zacharias, Simeon), closes in the temple with the disciples praising God — the whole Gospel framed in worship, ending where it began, in the house of God, with adoration. The witnesses note the pattern this sets for the Church: a life of continual worship, joy, and prayerful waiting for the Spirit's power — the disciples' practice the model for the believer, whose life is to be one of praise and dependence. The expositors observe the "Amen" that closes the Gospel: the seal of assent and certainty, the fitting close to the record of the Saviour's life, death, resurrection, and ascension — so be it, and so it is. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to live, like the disciples, in continual worship and joy, awaiting and depending on the Spirit's power; and to join the praise that crowns the Gospel of the risen and ascended Lord. The verse thus records the joyful close — the disciples continually praising God in the temple — teaching the continual worship and joy of the waiting Church, the fitting end of the Gospel in praise, and the days of mourning ended in the joy of the ascended Christ.
